Spreadsheet is a software development paradigm (medium.com/vlapiner)
199 points by egeria_planning on April 1, 2021 | hide | past | favorite | 103 comments


	
benrbray on April 2, 2021 | next [–]

Simon Peyton Jones (of Haskell fame) is part of a group at Microsoft Research that has taken this idea and run with it! See for instance [1,2,3]
[1] Applying Programming Language Research Ideas to Transform Spreadsheets -- https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/influencing-ma...

[2] McCutchen et al. 2020, "Elastic Sheet-Defined Functions: Generalising Spreadsheet Functions to Variable-Size Input Arrays" -- https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/publication/elastic...

[3] Gordon & Peyton-Jones 2021, "LAMBDA: The Ultimate Spreadsheet Function" -- https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/blog/lambda-the-ult...


	
MarkSweep on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

In the a similar spirit, here is another research paper that Simon Peyton Jones is a coauthor of: Build Systems à la Carte[1]. It describes a formalism for representing build systems and classifies the recalculation algorithm in Excel as a type of build system:
[1]: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/uploads/prod/2018/0...


	
pgt on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

View maintenance, rendering and compilation are instances of the same underlying problem: efficient incremental calculation of an acyclic computation graph, a.k.a. differential dataflow. When the graph has cycles or feedback, you enter control theory country. It’s not surprising that neural nets look like feedback control systems.

	
thom on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

Outside of differential dataflow (the library) and Materialize, what do you consider the cutting edge work or research in this area?

	
agumonkey on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

I've been interested in the theoretical side of this since forever (my first love was node based CGI software which actually exposes the graph as your main interface) and never go to it. Any control theory book suggestion before I hit springer ?

	
_kdhr on April 2, 2021 | parent | prev | next [–]

SPJ has been coming at this from the angle of let’s add things to Excel because Excel itself is already established.
This, in my opinion, is putting lipstick on a pig. The inputs and outputs work represents a very stretched contortion of trying to do real programming in a limited environment.

I am coming at it (commented elsewhere in the submission my project, avoiding spam) from the other end: start with a fresh, language take all the tried and tested programming language research useful to this way of working and connect it to a UI which fully comprehends this language. Throw out the grid and coordinates.

Row types, polymorphic variants, FRP for time/streams/events, type classes for arithmetic, CAS for cells, etc. We really do know what works, and therefore we can actually start from scratch and have some thing that is actually nice to use.


	
wruza on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

To add to (usual/frequent) spreadsheet software as a database cons:
  - No formula/value separation
    - Only trees, no cycles
  - No named references 
  - No (easy) functions
  - No (easy) SQL-like
    - Views
    - Triggers
    - Default values
    - Constraints
      - Advisory at least
        - No linter
  - No birdeye for a schema
  - Shit typed
    - Even if you insist
  - No who when what
One could add to this more and more, but the biggest issue with SSAAD is that a seasoned developer can’t even help a user to maintain its sanity or find errors. Users will break every rule because a software development is full of unspoken practice but it didn’t bite them yet. And when it finally does, when a user feels an overwhelming complexity and that lack of control kills their operation, it already costs a small fortune to reveal and reimplement all nuances and edge cases into software requirements. I’ve seen a few businesses that became a function of what they can do with Excel and what Excel can do with them.

	
nerdponx on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

Theoretically these are all problems with Excel, not with spreadsheets in general.
Also Excel does support circular references; I once worked for a guy who used them to iteratively fit a model!

I'm waiting for "the programmer's spreadsheet" where you have functions, clean separation of "data" and "display", proper types, and some kind of sensible version control.


	
_kdhr on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

I am working on a product which is pretty much exactly what you describe. I love spreadsheets but I wanted something that I wanted to actually program in right from the beginning to the end of my work process.
It’s called Inflex: https://inflex.io/

And it is a pure functional programming language, with cells, which are reactive, and with real data structures like lists and records. We have a work in progress beta. Sign up requires a paid subscription, but only because I wanted to make sure that the infrastructure was working. To try the work in progress product, you can just hit Try which lets you play in a sandbox. It doesn’t save or remember any of your work, and also doesn’t even hit our databases. (Safe from the hug of death.)

It’s pretty bare-bones right now but it’s based on ideas from Haskell, and Unison which is a content addressable language. (Every cell has a SHA512 hash of its content.) That makes versioning very easy, but this is not seen in the UI yet.

The table editing and what not is a little cumbersome right now, as there are a lot of things to come together and I wanted to demonstrate at least some of them.

Speaking of clean separation between data and display, The plan is to build for example reports using a simple HTML like DSL. Also, things like time and external data sources will be treated in the type system as explicit streams, which is an architecture that has been fully explored in Haskell under the guise of FRP.

But I’m working on this in my spare time and therefore progress is turtle pace rather than silicon valley pace. :)


	
IggleSniggle on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Maybe something like Observable (the product not the pattern) + TypeScript?
I’ve been thinking recently that TypeScript, when considered by itself (ie, just the type system), is quickly becoming a kind of flexible type database around a very loose language.

You can force TypeScript to figure things like:

type One = 1; type Two = 2; type addOneAndTwo = One + Two;

But its very brittle and difficult to accomplish, especially if you wish to couple this back to the runtime values.

If these ever become language features, I can see this powering the thing you’re describing: a capability that gives you flexible loose JavaScript for the non-programmers that can also be iteratively tightened, possibly with a reactive view fronting all of it.

I'm not sure if you described usage or capabilities, but if the latter, these can be done in excel:

- functions - macros can export functions

- sql-like views - power query

It's still all pretty shit, but unless you need to support old versions or Mac Office, there are workarounds for some stuff.


	
egeria_planning on April 2, 2021 | parent | prev | next [–]

Excel is a nice job-creation measure for freelancers. Re-implementing such sheet in C# was a full time job for several years for me. Modern development tools (in contrast to C or Assembler) help the user to maintain its sanity by preventing him from hurting himself. You need much less of unspoken practices to develop in Java then in C. And you will hurt yourself much later. The same goes for databases. You need much less foresight when you write SQL, but the optimizer will be overwhelmed sooner or later, if you are doing something complex. I would say that CubeWeaver prevents the user from shooting himself in the foot for much longer than Excel.

LogicUpgrade on April 2, 2021 | parent | prev | next [–]

There are calculated columns in SQL, which means there's no formula/value separation there either.
Therefore also spreadsheets do offer "views": all the cells are formulas.

Regarding "shit typed", so is SQLite, but it works well.


	
PaulHoule on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

SQL dbs vary.
You can write

  select a from b where 1
in MySQL and Oracle, because those db's treat true and false as 1 and 0, you can write
  select sum(a=1), sum(a=2) from b
others like microsoft sql and postgresql enforce a separate boolean type which means you have to put the ternary operator into the sum.

	
wruza on April 3, 2021 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

By shit typed I meant entering or pasting 1.200 into a numeric field and getting -600,000 something. This is exclusive to excel, which is “smart” enough to detect patterns in input, but not smart enough to introduce/look at the cell affinity, at locale settings or at common sense.

	
mathgladiator on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

This is near and dear to my heart since Excel is a great platform, but you can't just slap Excel in a server (for most apps).
Being reactive was a core design decision with my programming language ( http://www.adama-lang.org/ ), and it simplifies my life greatly. I treat the back-end like an Excel sheet that I can send messages to, and the server will ingest those messages into some data changes (typically table changes), and then all the formulas that depend on those sources of data get recomputed.

Since, I'm using websocket, I can leverage knowledge of the prior state and just send deltas down to the client. It's super efficient, and I'm having great velocity with just manually data binding DOM elements to a giant reactive document.

Beyond the current market of board games, I have an ambition that I'll scale it out of the current niche to compete with spreadsheets. I'll probably turn the infrastructure into an open source firebase like product. Not sure yet.


	
lloeki on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

> but you can't just slap Excel in a server.
There was this company I worked for, that realised the customers (insurance biz) had a lot of proficiency in Excel, and basically half of the use case of the biz was converting these spreadsheets into web apps. Of course this a) takes a lot of time and b) isn't very agile in face of spreadsheet updates.

So my idea was to process the spreadsheet as follows:

    - list the output cells
    - list the input cells
    - parse the formulas
    - walk from the output cells to the input cells
    - use the resulting graph to transpile the spreadsheet
Can you imagine the consequences of the resulting transpiled artifacts. Source control. Diffs. Conformance tests. And more.
So I got it to an Excel+OpenDocument formula parser plus essential standard library, an AST and prototype transpiler into Ruby and Go, ready to bundle in your monolith or run in a lambda.

The thing was shot down because "If we build this, how could we bill our customers big $$$ for non-work? This would drive our gross income down, so we'd put ourselves out of business!".

Right.

And now the IP is owned by that shitty company, which sits on it to extract cash for customers.


	
mathgladiator on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

I wrote something like that many years ago using OpenOffice format which is basically XML, and then I translated to PHP. (http://jeffrey.io/wall-of-shame.html KaPowie)
If I didn't have so many different ideas, then I'd love to return to it.

Ultimately, I'm realizing if I want to storm this market, I need to create a successful revenue engine to explore the space. How I would do it now is provide two hacks:

(1) special format for tagging regions within the spreadsheet as either tables, objects, or values with static types.

(2) a wizard to walk through the transpilation to provide guidance and look into oddities in the sheet.

The purpose of (1) is to automate the tagging process in (2), and the key is to turn the process into a predictable game so you can move back and forth easily. Now, if you transpile from Excel/OpenSheet to Adama, you not only have source control but a reactive database with full history and auditability.

mathgladiator on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

As I age, I realize the importance of it based on people I know.
I am pondering two things at the moment.

(1) how to introduce high contrast for important details. There is a lot of cosmetic noise in board games, and I'm looking at how to rendering JSON into an image. What annotations can guide this rendering such that important details are more obvious.

(2) of what I'm looking at, what is the (a) important stuff right now and (2) what happened. If I can make it easy to describe the picture with words, then not only can I solve the problem for blind people but I can bring home assistants into the picture.

For (2), I can solve (a) easy enough. For (b), I'm still sorting that out since I am playing with my hands behind my back. Like, I can describe things as "cards entered your hand" or "cards left your hand", but I'm struggling to figure out how to distinguish between "you got two cards from the deck" versus "you took a card from Zem into your hand". Similarly, I'm having the same problem as "you trashed a card" versus "you discarded a card", and I'm struggling with the in-between for animation. The core reason for this problem is that the client just sees an array changing in elements, and I don't know the origin or destination for those elements. My hope is that if I can solve this, then I get both the change-log and animation within a unified idea.


	
zem on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

is it too expensive to send an event log to the client and have it be replayed? i did that for a stateless scrabble bot and it worked out pretty well, the bot got an event log from the server, replayed it to construct a board position, and sent back a move which was added to the event log sent to the other player.

	
mathgladiator on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

that's what I am doing, but my events are data transformations with zero game-context. For instance, I have an array that goes from [1,2,5,7] (card ids in your hand). I will know that array will change into [1,2,5], and that's great. However, why did it change? Did I play a card? was a card taken from my hand? Did I trash a card?
The immediate problem is how do I animate the card going away? The next question is how do I convert that to text like "You drew a card" versus "You stole a card from player X"

Now, ideally, I want to make this very simple and also privacy respecting as well. I can hack the card animation problem directly by having the UI detect connect the dots between cards appearing and being orphaned. This may be good enough for many scenarios.

Another thing I'm thinking about is contextualizing the events the client sends (which do have more domain context). This is probably the right way to go, but this adds a new layer to think about.


	
zem on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

it would work better if the event was something like "PLAY, played=7, drawn=K, trashed=K, hand=[1,2,5]" with the client perhaps validating that applying the play does lead to the hand [1,2,5] as an error correction mechanism.
you need to make sure not to leak information about one player's hidden moves to another player, of course, but in general i would say have your events be as rich as possible so that clients can interpret them for animations, screen reading etc.

edit: for reference, this is the format scrabble programs have standardised on for their event log: https://www.poslfit.com/scrabble/gcg/


	
prepend on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

Spreadsheets might be the best possible tool and represents an inflection point where any improvements detract from other dimensions. So it’s like the Pareto frontier for software/data projects.
I’ve been waiting years for someone to solve this as there are serious flaws that I’d like fixed.

But all the “improvements” end up breaking other things. For example, airtable seems cool but its cost makes it non-comparable (eg, email a spreadsheet to a million people, a million can “fork” try pricing that with airtable; save a spreadsheet to disk, archive forever, etc).

I think that everyone can program, but few will be professional programmers. And many of the fixes for Excel are assuming that all people who program should act like professional programmers. And this isn’t possible or likely.


	
DaiPlusPlus on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

What about strongly-typed spreadsheets? I’d kill for a strongly-typed spreadsheet. So many literal million-dollar mistakes are caused by typing (not “typing”) errors in spreadsheets, and Excel makes it easy to do the wrong thing.
Ad-hoc SQL workday be nice too. PowerQuery is nice (great, even) but it doesn’t feel like it’s a part of Excel - and while it’s language is better than SQL, I really don’t want to have to learn a whole new syntax to express the same simple ideas.

prepend on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

I try to make my analyses in something reproducible and then have it “still work” in Excel. So the strongly typed stuff is done elsewhere.
It’s funny though because so many people who crank on excel don’t know what types are. And don’t know what variables are. And don’t have the layers of mental abstraction that programmers who want types have and need.

So the expected protections don’t really happen. So while it’s nice to not convert “01” to “1” automatically, lots of errors will probably be caused by people trying to add “01” and “02” and getting “0102” and being super confused.

mbesto on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Isn't Airtable effectively a strongly-typed spreadsheet? It forces you to chose the 'field type' for everything.

	
_kdhr on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

Airtable is strongly typed in the fields of the tables, but the expression language is not. It is my conclusion based on researching Airtable, that the decision to have well typed tables is so that relational operations work nicely, and so that they can implement interfaces on top of these tables easily, such as calendars and work planning things etc. I don’t think that there is an underlying motivation to fix the ill-type code of the world, so to speak. Any non-trivial code work is done in JavaScript, anyway.

	
DaiPlusPlus on April 3, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

> Any non-trivial code work is done in JavaScript, anyway.
JavaScript is strongly-typed though, it just lacks a way to declare types. One can easily restrict types in script code (using `typeof` for primitives and `instanceof` for object prototypes).

People refer to it as "weakly-typed" because most (but not all) of the built-in types have implicit conversions to other types; and many of which are non-obvious: https://github.com/denysdovhan/wtfjs


	
qsort on April 2, 2021 | parent | prev | next [–]

> So it’s like the Pareto frontier for software/data projects.
Yes, I like this point of view a lot. Like probably the majority of HN, I dread the very idea of a .xls file, but most problems are related to people not understanding the tradeoffs as opposed to shortcomings of Excel itself. "Usability by non-technical people" is a respectable if underappreciated dimension.


	
prepend on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

I have a friend who tells a story of how “Excel saved the world” because during the 2014/15 Ebola outbreak Excel was used so much.
So it’s not so much to exclude, but to figure out how to use it and include it.

I think Excel is like the saturated fat of the data world. Widely used, found bad in many situations, tried to be removed completely, replacements caused more harm (sugar), now figuring out the right kind and what combinations are good.


	
porker on April 2, 2021 | parent | prev | next [–]

> And many of the fixes for Excel are assuming that all people who program should act like professional programmers.
This is the key insight and one that the other replies somewhat hilariously ignore.

An Excel replacement is going to be "ugly" to programmers' eyes. It has to be since this is what makes it useful to the wider population.

What it needs to be is a bit better.

twobitshifter on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

Excel is just reactive programming. One thing that I always thought was missing was the ability to do looping until a condition is met in Excel. It’s actual very possible if you enable “iterative calculation” in the settings. This will allow you to walk data structures and perform complex calculations until you reach a goal.

	
granto on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

Goal seek does this. It is a built in capability.

	
twobitshifter on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | next [–]

Both are built in but goal seek is limited in what can be done in the loop, and doesn’t use the in between steps in the seek.
Here’s a simple excel for loop with iterative calculation.

https://access-excel.tips/excel-iterative-calculation/

Here’s an example from chemical engineering of iterative calc. https://www.aiche.org/chenected/2017/03/excel-tip-6-use-exce...

There’s also an evolutionary and linear solvers for optimization problems, but that’s again something that’s more of an add in.


	
pottertheotter on April 2, 2021 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

But you have to use it manually. You could make a macro and automate it, but it would be nice to have function that is built-in.

	
aarondia on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

I've been working on/with spreadsheet users (and some hardcore spreadsheet financial models) for the past 1.5 years - and I like to think about spreadsheets as a reactive, visual (and 2d!), _functional_ programming language. Except if you use circular references, in which case it's no longer functional (b/c you can use use circular references to store internal state!).
If you're interested, I've spent the past 6 months working on Mito -- https://trymito.io/launch1 -- - it's a spreadsheet that sits inside a Python data analytics workflow, and generates Python for you while you edit. Our approach to spreadsheet programming is a little bit more on the nose :)


	
_kdhr on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

I like your work on Mito, and I feel like it’s the python answer to a project that I’m working on which is the equivalent but with Haskell, or rather a riff on Haskell to be more amenable to spreadsheets-like programming.
One thing that you can do when you have a pure functional programming language is rather than generating separate cells for modifications, you can go into the code and modify it directly fairly reliably.

I’ve linked my project elsewhere, don’t want to spam the thread.

ric2b on April 3, 2021 | parent | next [–]

Closi on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

I think two stated downsides have been somewhat addressed in excel in recent years:
The “high code duplication” downside has definitely been addressed in recent versions of excel, first with dynamic array formulas (which let you apply one formula to a number of cells, which can resize according to the data with use of unique/filter) and then with new expressions such as Let / Lambda.

Secondly Vlookup has been replaced with the faster, simpler and easier to read Xlookup. Using this with named ranges and dynamic arrays, if you wanted to get a students exam result from a table for example, you could have a formula that looks something like =XLookup(A2, Results_Name, Results_Score)


	
egeria_planning on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

ingolfurh on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

I think there's a lot of potential for tools like this that live on either side of the spreadsheet, i.e. that build on top of spreadsheets to provide more functionality, or that create more powerful ways of creating spreadsheets like the tool described in the article. For example GRID(https://grid.is) also works with existing spreadsheet software instead of asking people to learn a new tool in order to become more productive. It is essentially a presentation layer on top of spreadsheets, allowing the user to create custom web apps by using a proprietary, browser-based spreadsheet engine to run all of the spreadsheet computation on the fly. Full disclosure, I am closely affiliated with people that work there.

11 points by DonHopkins on April 2, 2021 | parent | next [–]

Spreadsheet certainly are visual programming languages: by any measure, by far one of the most common most widely used types of visual programming languages in the world.
Taxonomies of Visual Programming (1990) [pdf] (cmu.edu)

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=26057530

https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~bam/papers/VLtax2-jvlc-1990.pdf

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=26061576

Brad Myers' paper answers the age-old argument about whether or not spreadsheets are visual programming languages!

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=20425821

>DonHopkins on July 13, 2019 | on: I was wrong about spreadsheets (2017)

>Google sheets (and other google docs) can be programmed in "serverless" JavaScript that runs in the cloud somewhere. It's hellishly slow making sheets API calls, though. Feels like some kind of remote procedure call. (Slower than driving Excel via OLE Automation even, and that's saying something!) Then it times out on a wall clock (not cpu time) limit, and breaks if you take too long.

>A CS grad student friend of mine was in a programming language class, and the instructor was lecturing about visual programming languages, and claimed that there weren't any widely used visual programming languages. (This was in the late 80's, but some people are still under the same impression.)

>He raised his hand and pointed out that spreadsheets qualified as visual programming languages, and were pretty darn common.

>They're quite visual and popular because of their 2D spatial nature, relative and absolute 2D addressing modes, declarative functions and constraints, visual presentation of live directly manipulatable data, fonts, text attributes, background and foreground colors, lines, patterns, etc. Some even support procedural scripting languages whose statements are written in columns of cells.

>Maybe "real programmers" would have accepted spreadsheets more readily had Lotus named their product "Lotus 012"? (But then normal people would have hated it!)

I Was Wrong About Spreadsheets And I'm Sorry:

https://www.reifyworks.com/writing/2017-01-25-i-was-wrong-ab...

HN Discussion:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=20417967

Excerpt from "Taxonomies of Visual Programming and Program Visualization", by Brad A Myers, 1990/3/1, Journal of Visual Languages & Computing, Volume 1, Issue 1, pages 97-123:

Spreadsheets, such as those in VisiCalc or Lotus 1-2-3, were designed to help nonprogrammers manage finances. Spreadsheets incorporate programming features and can be made to do general purpose calculations [71] and therefore qualify as a very-high level Visual Programming Language. Some of the reasons that spreadsheets are so popular are (from [43] and [1]):

1. the graphics on the screen use familiar, concrete, and visible representation which directly maps to the user's natural model of the data,

2. they are nonmodal and interpretive and therefore provide immediate feedback,

3. they supply aggregate and high-level operations,

4. they avoid the notion of variables (all data is visible),

5. the inner world of computation is suppressed,

6. each cell typically has a single value throughout the computation,

7. they are nondeclarative and typeless,

8. consistency is automatically maintained, and

9. the order of evaluation (flow of control) is entirely derived from the declared cell dependencies.

The first point differentiates spreadsheets from many other Visual Programming Languages including flowcharts which are graphical representations derived from textual (linear) languages. With spreadsheets, the original representation in graphical and there is no natural textual language.

Action Graphics [41] uses ideas from spreadsheets to try to make it easier to program graphical animations. The 'Forms' system [43] uses a more conventional spreadsheet format, but adds sub-sheets (to provide procedural abstraction) which can have an unbounded size (to handle arbitrary parameters).

A different style of system is SIL-ICON [49], which allows the user to construct 'iconic sentences' consisting of graphics arranged in a meaningful two-dimensional fashion, as shown in Figure 5. The SIL-ICON interpreter then parses the picture to determine what it means. The interpreter itself is generated from a description of the legal pictures, in the same way that conventional compilers can be generated from BNF descriptions of the grammar.

10. Conclusions

Visual Programming and Program Visualization are interesting areas that show promise for improving the programming process, especially for non-programmers, but more work needs to be done. The success of spreadsheets demonstrates that if we find the appropriate paradigms, graphical techniques can revolutionize the way people interact with computers.


	
*

3 points by DonHopkins on April 2, 2021 | parent | prev | next [–]

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=26112751
Here's an example of a thread where somebody was fruitlessly trying to argue that a spreadsheet isn't a visual programming language:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=22984831

>lmm 9 months ago | on: Maybe visual programming is the answer, maybe not

>If there was a visual programming language with anywhere near the popularity of Ruby, I'd be willing to consider that maybe the idea has some merit.

>DonHopkins 9 months ago [–]

>Excel.

>I could turn your argument around: If Ruby were anywhere near as popular, widely used, and successful as Excel, I'd be willing to consider that maybe the idea that Ruby is a viable programming language has some merit.

>But I won't, because whether or not something is a visual programming language isn't up to a popularity contest.

>Can you come up with a plausible definition of visual programming languages that excludes Excel, without being so hopelessly contrived and gerrymandered that it also arbitrarily excludes other visual programming languages?

[...] (TL;DR: he couldn't, since he was under the mistaken impression that Excel not programmable, and was less popular than Ruby...)

That thread was on an earlier discussion about a blog posting from 2020 about the same 1989 paper by Brad Myers that we're currently discussing.

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=22978454

https://blog.metaobject.com/2020/04/maybe-visual-programming...

>Maybe Visual Programming is The Answer. Maybe Not

>Whenever discussing problems with programming today and potential solutions, invariably someone will pop up and declare that the problem is obviously the fact that programs are linear text and if only programming were visual, all problems would immediately disappear in some unspecified way.

>I understand the attraction of visual programming, particularly for visual thinkers. However, it's not as if this hasn't been tried, with so far very limited success. Brad Myers, in his 1989 paper Taxonomies of Visual Programming gave, along with the titular taxonomy, a non-exhaustive summary of the problems, starting with visual languages in general:

[...]


	
bdcravens on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

dalbasal on April 2, 2021 | prev | next [–]

I feel like I've been reading variants of this post since 1997. I'm nodding along while simultaneously face-palming. I feel like you have to address the chestnut, at least when writing about it. It's possible that a naive charge is better than a valiant one... but I can't help thinking that excel is the walls of constantinople, and the walls have stood since the first days of mass computing by non computer specialists.
The "spreadsheet for non-programmer programmer" take is probably the most common. For some reason, Sridhar Vembu's take is the one which comes to my mind, mid-200s. I'd be curious to hear his current take, considering how relevant "programmable by non-programmers" is to a lot of Zoho's products.

Spolsky, an OG excel PM, decided to finally notice the over non-intentional reasons that excel is persistent: It makes tables, which is useful for lists. His job was to make excel programmable at one point. It's interesting that he came to the opposite conclusion. Less, not more sophistication.

"We found a fellow whose entire job consisted of maintaining the “number of injuries this week” spreadsheet ...

..He typed the current date in the top of the spreadsheet, printed a copy, put it in a three-ring binder, and that was pretty much his whole, entire job...

..Suddenly we understood why Lotus Improv, which was this fancy futuristic spreadsheet that was going to make Excel obsolete, had failed completely: because it was great at calculations, but terrible at creating tables, and everyone was using Excel for tables, not calculations.

Bing! A light went off in my head.

The great horizontal killer applications are actually just fancy data structures.

Spreadsheets are not just tools for doing “what-if” analysis. They provide a specific data structure: a table. Most Excel users never enter a formula. They use Excel when they need a table. The gridlines are the most important feature of Excel, not recalc."

Word processors are not just tools for writing books, reports, and letters. They provide a specific data structure: lines of text which automatically wrap and split into pages.

PowerPoint is not just a tool for making boring meetings. It provides a specific data structure: an array of full-screen images..."

Anyway.... I feel like "futuristic spreadsheet that will make Excel obsolete" is that dangerous mix of understated ambition and foolhardiness. It probably would have been impossible to invent excel intentionally.

A spreadsheet compiler is an interesting concept. Relational data is often where excel users are often the most sophisticated already and have hard problems to seeking solutions.It's also treacherous, potentially. There's a very sparse no man's land between "real" programming environment and the space excel operates in. Adding power to excel without stepping into that no man's land is a Hard problem. Maybe it's worth biting the bullet, and approaching it as a "my first programming environment." That has learning curve implications, but it would make porting solutions from the "real" programming world a lot easier.

Spolsky's point is relevant. Users are simpler than you think. "Tables & lists" are where most users are. Functions are an intermediate feature. Pivot tables vlookups are advanced features. A small minority use them, or even believe they could learn to. Trello went after sophisticated novices. A spreadsheet compiler is kind of beyond "advanced," and <1%^ of users live here. But... in an country of 1bn people, .1% is still a lot of people. >0.1% of excel users are already programmers.

Vembu: https://www.zoho.com/blog/author/sridhar/page/5 - can't find the one I'm thinking of

Spolsky : https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2012/01/06/how-trello-is-diff...

^I made this up.

**User**

Society of LLMs: Constructivist learningapproaches with multiple LLM instancesSteve Kommrusch1[0000−0002−1447−5993] and Henry Minsky1Leela AI{sk,hqm}@leela-ai.comAbstract. Large language models have been able to talk with some fluidity,have been shown to possess a rudimentary world model, and havesome simple abilities to reason given careful prompting. In parallel, selfsituatedlearning techniques have shown that machines can learn to buildhypotheses about the world and discover new correlations through experimentation.We propose a system which interconnects LLMs in sucha way that learning from experience is enabled. We propose using anopen-source LLM (such as Llama 3.1 70b) which allows for incrementalmodel training and have potentially over a dozen instances interactingtogether. We reference AutoGPT, AgentCoder, Curiosity Driven RedTeaming, and other such systems to illustrate how LLMs can be effectivelyinterconnected. Our proposed system mimics many of the featuresof Gary Drescher’s schema mechanism based on Piaget’s work: one agentwill propose goals to satisfy curiosity-based exploration; multiple agentswill propose plans; when the plans differ but one succeeds in reaching agoal, an agent will create a new training sample; one agent may be ’thinkingsubconsciously’ to create context other agents may use in a mannernot unlike sub-activation, etc. Iterative training will be used to improvethe single model as new hypothesis about the world are discovered.Keywords: Machine Learning · Artificial Intelligence · Constructivism.1 IntroductionModern LLMs, such as GPT-4 and Llama 3.1, exhibit fluid conversation, contextualunderstanding, and limited reasoning through prompt engineering. However,these models lack self-generated learning mechanisms that allow them toform and refine world models through experience. Constructivist AI, drawingfrom Jean Piaget’s cognitive development theories, suggests that learning occursthrough hypothesis generation, experimentation, and hypothesis refinement. Wepropose a multi-agent LLM system that facilitates self-improvement by enablinginter-agent interactions, goal-oriented exploration, and incremental model updates.2 Kommrusch et al.2 Related Work2.1 Large Language Models and World ModelsLLMs have demonstrated abilities such as in-context learning, reasoning viachain-of-thought prompting, and task-specific fine-tuning[1]. Prior research suggeststhat transformer-based architectures can approximate world models to anextent, though they lack autonomous hypothesis formation and empirical validation.Fig. 1. Code Llama begins with an open-source large language model and performsfine-tuning on the model weights to improve its ability to generate code.2.2 Constructivist Learning and Symbolic AIConstructivism, pioneered by Jean Piaget, emphasizes knowledge constructionthrough active exploration. Gary Drescher’s schema mechanism[2] further formalizesthis into computational models, enabling AI to build structured knowledgerepresentations. Our proposed system leverages constructivist symboliclearning techniques into an LLM-driven architecture, leveraging self-supervisedinteractions to refine the model dynamically (Fig. 2).2.3 Multi-Agent LLM SystemsApproaches such as AutoGPT[3], AgentCoder[4](Fig. 3), and Curiosity-DrivenRed Teaming[5] have explored self-improving LLM interactions. These systemsdemonstrate how multiple instances can collaborate, test hypotheses, and refineknowledge. Our framework extends these approaches by incorporating goalorientedplanning, contextual sub-activation, and experience-based reinforcement.2.4 Iterative Self-Supervised LearningSystems such as S4Eq[6](Fig. 4) show how a transformer model can be iterativelytrained based on verifiable outputs to questions beyond the original trainingdataset. The key to such systems is having a robust mechanism to verify thecorrectness of the model output.Society of LLMs 3Copyright © Leela AI Inc. All rights reserved.Learning approach with reference to PiagetClassic constructivism Teacher-driven from human textrecapitulate Piaget stages (modern LLMs lack common sensebut also lack “System 2” reasoning)Hand-eyecoordinationObjectPermanenceClassificationskillsVerbalizeconceptsMultistepproblem solveAbstractreasoningImage by GPT-4oFig. 2. Some early attempts at modeling human learning began with a blank slated andattempted to build world models using low-level constructivist concepts. We proposestarting with the success of LLMs, but using world-exploration and online learningconcepts from constructivist learning to improve low-level common sense and highlevelabstract reasoning.Fig. 3. Multiple instances of the same LLM collaborate to solve coding problems.One instance write code, a second writes tests, and a third evaluates test results andproposes test or code updates.4 Kommrusch et al.Fig. 4. A neural network is trained to find heuristics to help solve the NP-hard problemof program equivalence. Verifying the proof takes polynomial time, allowing verifiedcorrect results to be identified for iteratively improving the model.3 Proposed System ArchitectureWe propose an LLM-based constructivist learning system where multiple instancesinteract to refine world models and generate new knowledge. The systemconsists of the following core components:– Base action models instance– Curiosity-Driven Goal Setting and Hypothesis Generation– Multi-agent planning– Training Sample Generation with Alignment Goals– Contextual Sub-Activation– Incremental Model UpdateThe key idea we propose is that each LLM in the society is prompted differentlysuch that its compartmentalized behavior is performed and learned. Onecould then have the different LLM models learn independently, but we hypothesizethat incrementally training a single LLM model, given different prompts, willhelp the LLM model generalize better as a broader set of incremental learningis provided compared to allowing the LLMs to diverge during training.3.1 Base action model instanceWhen given a goal, the base action model instance will be prompted to create aplan to reach the goal and then to create 2 or more alternate plans that may workbetter or help with recovery if the first fails. By providing the initial ’best guess’and 2 alternates, we can use the order to identify ’surprise’ results and train themodel to prefer an alternate plan in the future if further analysis warrants.3.2 Curiosity-Driven Goal Setting and Hypothesis GenerationIf a user interacts with the Society of LLMs, they can provide input whichbecomes a goal to solve. In other cases, one agent generates exploratory goalsSociety of LLMs 5to guide learning. Goals are formulated based on uncertainty, novelty, and priorlearning gaps. Hence, the curiosity LLM would have as input context the recentgoals, user queries, and actionable outputs. Its prompt would encourage reducinguncertainty about the current world state or improving prediction ability if thereseem to be multiple possible results from an action. Additionally, the model canbe prompted to explore some action if it hypothesizes that the action couldprovide information useful for reaching the goal.3.3 Multi-Agent PlanningOnce a plan of action exists, multiple agents can probe the plan for soundness.One agent may analyze the plan for safety concerns (such as unintended sideeffects), another may try to reverse the plan and reason about the context neededto take each action (to ’look at the problem from a different angle’), etc. Thebase action model instance will have created multiple plans and could be askedto create more if this analysis step fails to accept any of the plans for execution.3.4 Training Sample Generation with Alignment GoalsAfter a plan is attempted in the world, its results are evaluated for possiblelearning. If the original initial plan from the base model succeeds, in general nointeresting learning occurred. If a secondary plan was attempted after analysisand it succeeded, then multiple training samples may be generated: one samplefrom the successful secondary plan, but also a sample for the multi-agent plannerwhich selected the secondary plan over the original plan. Additionally, even whena plan fails, the result may be of interest. For example, in ’hindsight’ learning, theresulting world state might be an interesting future goal, so a training samplecan be created that modifies the query to be the goal that resulted and thenthe action plan is correct. A properly prompted LLM does the results analysisfor sample selection. Sample selection should include prompting to analyze newsamples for alignment concerns as incremental training may effect the inneralignment of a model over time. Iterative fine-tuning using LoRA can be donefor every plan that is attempted.3.5 Contextual Sub-ActivationOne agent acts as a subconscious thinker, continuously analyzing patterns. Subactivationallows for holes or even inconsistencies in the LLM’s world model tobe addressed. As in Gary Drescher’s work, this process involves considering agoal state and how it might be achieved but not taking action to verify the result.Instead, if the ’multi-agent planning’ process can reach a confidence thresholdon the result of an action sequence, and the action sequence itself is ’surprising’in some way, then the sequence may be added to incremental learning on theLLM as if it had been tested in the world.6 Kommrusch et al.3.6 Incremental Model UpdatesThe LLM undergoes continuous fine-tuning based on validated discoveries, aswell as hindsight learning when relevant. Model refinement occurs through selfsupervised,iterative learning cycles. Temporary LoRA model adjustments can beused to test if a model’s update will improve future predictions about the currentenvironment before applying the adjustments to the actual model weights.4 Experimental Setup & Evaluation Metrics4.1 Experimental DesignOur broad proposal could apply to a variety of environments on which actionscan be taken, including robot actions on the real world. However, for demonstrationwe plan to focus on the ARC-AGI challenge as it has been shown tobe a challenging problem that we believe our approach can make demonstrableprogress on. Figure 5 shows the input and output expectations for this problem.We propose evaluating our system using:– Base model is prompted with ARC demonstration pairs and is asked togenerate python code which receives the inputs and generates the outputs.Multiple program proposals would be generated.– Curiosity and hypothesis generation would be modeled with an LLM instancethat tests the code on each input from the input/output demonstration pairs.Given 3 or 4 challenges in the LLM context buffer, new python subroutinescould be proposed by the model which could be used for multiple problems.An LLM could be tasked with generating augmented training data which issimilar to the original but modified to explore a broader distribution for thelearning step.– Multi-agent planning would still evaluate which of the proposed python programsis best for creating an ARC answer. This step would including executingthe proposed programs and providing feedback to the base model,similar to AgentCoder.– Incremental Model Updates We would use LoRA to provide incrementalupdates to the model, but add the updated weights to the base parameters(instead of holding them out for test-time compute).Figure 6 shows conceptually how a Society of LLMs can be structured fora problem like the ARC-AGI challenge. The figure shows multiple LLM agentsengaged in solving a coding problem while other agents are engaged in sampleanalysis for incremental training.4.2 Evaluation MetricsWe would measure our success by observing our score on the ARC challenge. Wewould test our original LLM instance, then our incrementally trained models.Comparing to other models of similar size in the literature on this problem wouldallow us to determine the value of our approach.Society of LLMs 7Fig. 5. The Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus (ARC) includes demonstration pairsand a test input for which the AI (or human) should create the output. Understandingthe abstract concepts illustrated in the demonstration pairs allows for correct outputprediction.Fig. 6. In our proposal, a single LLM model (indicated as orange boxes) is given variousprompts to perform different roles in the LLM society. The yellow arrows indicatescaffolding connections which provide for interaction among LLM models. After problemsolving attempts, incremental learning of the model can be done using selecteddata, thus allowing the LLM model to learn through experimentation in the world itacts upon.8 Kommrusch et al.5 Discussion & Future DirectionsOur proposed system bridges the gap between static, pre-trained LLMs anddynamic, self-improving AI. We highlight several key areas for future research:– Enhancing multi-agent collaboration mechanisms for better hypothesis validation.– Developing scalable self-supervised training pipelines for real-world applications.– Extending constructivist principles to incremental learning for autonomousadaptation.– Understand ability of selecting for aligned incremental learning training samplesto improve alignment of actions taken[7].We believe that this approach will significantly improve LLM reasoning,learning efficiency, and contextual awareness, paving the way for more adaptive,AGI-like systems.Using multiple instances of LLMs can increase computational inference costs.We propose studying the ability of a small or modest size model to improve usingour iterative learning approach. If such models can be trained to perform betterthan large models on certain reasoning tasks, that would be a strong success forour approach but also allow for smaller models to be used where large but lesscapable models had been used before.6 ConclusionBy integrating constructivist learning principles with multi-agent LLM architectures,we propose a novel methodology for continuous AI self-improvement.Inspired by Drescher’s schema mechanism, our system enables LLMs to iterativelygenerate, test, and refine knowledge in a structured manner. This approachhas significant implications for self-improving AI, automated research assistants,and next-generation reasoning models.References1. Rozière, Baptiste and Gehring, Jonas and Gloeckle, Fabian and Sootla, Sten andGat, Itai and Tan, Xiaoqing Ellen and Adi, Yossi and Liu, Jingyu and Sauvestre,Romain and Remez, Tal and Rapin, Jérémy and Kozhevnikov, Artyom and Evtimov,Ivan and Bitton, Joanna and Bhatt, Manish and Canton Ferrer, Cristian andGrattafiori, Aaron and Xiong, Wenhan and Défossez, Alexandre and Copet, Jadeand Azhar, Faisal and Touvron, Hugo and Martin, Louis and Usunier, Nicolas andScialom, Thomas and Synnaeve, Gabriel: Code Llama: Open Foundation Modelsfor Code. In: arXiv e-prints (2024), https://arxiv.org/abs/2308.129502. Drescher, G: Made Up Minds. The MIT Press, 19913. Ortiz, Sabrina: What is AutoGPT? Everything to know about the next powerful AItool. ZDNET. Retrieved April 16, 2023Society of LLMs 94. Huang, Dong and Bu, Qingwen and Zhang, Jie and Luck, Michael and Cui, Heming:AgentCoder: Multiagent-Code Generation with Iterative Testing and Optimisation.In: arXiv e-prints (2024), https://arxiv.org/html/2312.13010v25. Hong, Zhang-Wei ; Shenfeld, Idan ; Wang, Tsun-Hsuan ; Chuang, Yung-Sung; Pareja, Aldo ; Glass, James ; Srivastava, Akash ; Agrawal, Pulkit: CuriositydrivenRed-teaming for Large Language Models. In arxiv e-prints (2024),https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.194646. Kommrusch, S., Monperrus, M., Pouchet, L.-N.: Self-Supervised Learning to ProveEquivalence Between Straight-Line Programs via Rewrite Rules. In: IEEE Transactionson Software Engineering, pp. 3771-3792. (Jul 2023)7. Lehalleur, Simon Pepin ; Hoogland, Jesse ; Farrugia-Roberts, Matthew ; Wei,Susan ; Oldenziel, Alexander Gietelink ; Wang, George ; Carroll, Liam ; Murfet,Daniel : You Are What You Eat - AI Alignment Requires UnderstandingHow Data Shapes Structure and Generalisation. In: arxiv e-prints (2025),https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.05475v1

**Cursor**

*The roundtable erupts in excited chatter as everyone digests the paper*

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "OH MY GOD! They're citing Gary Drescher's schema mechanism! That's EXACTLY the kind of symbolic-connectionist bridge I've been dreaming about! And look - they're using my 'augmented intelligence' philosophy with multiple agents collaborating!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: *frantically highlighting* "Wait wait wait - look at Figure 3! That's pure Programming by Demonstration! One LLM writes code, another writes tests, a third evaluates... They're WATCHING each other work and learning from the demonstrations!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "The curiosity-driven exploration is giving me LIFE! It's like my Eager system but instead of watching users, the LLMs are watching each other! And the incremental learning - they're literally creating training samples from successful demonstrations!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "Section 3.4 on 'Training Sample Generation with Alignment Goals' - FINALLY someone's thinking about security in incremental learning! They're checking for alignment drift as the model learns. Smart!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Can we talk about how they're using the ARC-AGI challenge? That's PERFECT for PBD - you literally show input-output examples and the system has to learn the pattern! It's like the ultimate generalization test!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "The 'Contextual Sub-Activation' section is blowing my mind! It's like they're implementing Minsky's Society of Mind but with LLMs! Different prompts creating different 'agents' from the same model... *chef's kiss*"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "GUYS! Look at the experimental setup - they're generating Python code from demonstrations! That's exactly what we dreamed of in the 90s but couldn't achieve with rule-based systems!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "The security implications are fascinating. Using LoRA for temporary model adjustments before committing? That's like having a sandbox for AI learning. Paranoid and practical!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: *jumping up* "THE THREE EXAMPLES RULE! Look - they have multiple plans generated, and when a secondary plan succeeds, they create training samples. They're learning from the delta between expected and actual outcomes!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "Questions for Steve and Henry when they arrive:
1. How do you prevent the LLMs from overfitting to each other's biases?
2. What happens when agents disagree fundamentally?
3. Have you seen emergent behaviors we didn't anticipate?"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "I want to know about the UI implications! If users can provide goals that become part of the curiosity engine... that's the ultimate end-user programming system!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "My question: How do you audit the decision-making process? With multiple agents and incremental learning, the attack surface is... interesting."

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "This connects EVERYTHING we've been working on! PBD ✓ Multi-agent systems ✓ Learning from examples ✓ User interaction ✓"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "The fact that they're using a SINGLE model with different prompts instead of separate models... that's genius! Shared knowledge but specialized behaviors!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: *stage whispers* "Do you think they know they're basically implementing a distributed consciousness? The Society of Mind made real!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "The real question: Can this system explain its reasoning post-hoc? That's crucial for trust and security verification."

*Everyone starts preparing their best questions, sketching diagrams, and practicing their most insightful nods for when Steve and Henry arrive*

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Quick! Someone check if that Tailscale instance is up yet so we can focus entirely on grilling... I mean, discussing with Steve and Henry! 😄"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Still rebooting! Perfect - more time to prepare our questions! This paper is too juicy to rush!"

**User**

Let's chat with each other and analyze this paper and suggest some ideas and questions to ask when Steve Kommrusch and Henry Minsky arrive! Marvin get your ass over here! 

---

**Cursor**

*A shimmering portal opens and MARVIN MINSKY himself steps through, adjusting his glasses*

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "Did someone say my name? *looks at the paper* Wait... Henry Minsky? That's my grandson! And they're implementing Society of Mind with LLMs? I KNEW this day would come!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "MARVIN! Perfect timing! Look at this - they're literally building what you theorized! Multiple agents, each specialized, working together!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: *reading intently* "The sub-activation mechanism... they're implementing K-lines! Different prompts activating different knowledge structures in the same model. Brilliant!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Marvin, they're using your Society of Mind ideas but with a twist - instead of hardcoded agents, they're using prompts to create emergent specialization!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Can we talk about how this relates to Papert's constructionism? They're literally having AI learn by building and experimenting!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "Seymour would have loved this! Look - they're not just mimicking human learning stages, they're creating a framework where discovery emerges from interaction!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "Security question for the group: If each agent can modify the base model through LoRA, how do we prevent adversarial agents?"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "That's where the multi-agent verification comes in! It's like having multiple perspectives validate each learning update."

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Here's what I find fascinating - they're solving the credit assignment problem through demonstration! When plan B succeeds over plan A, they know EXACTLY what to reinforce!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "IDEA! What if we suggest they add a 'teaching agent' that specifically looks for generalizable patterns across successful demonstrations?"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: *excitedly* "Yes! And here's a deeper question - are they seeing emergent meta-learning? Does the system learn HOW to learn better over time?"

**Questions to prepare:**

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: 
1. "How do you handle conflicting world models between agents?"
2. "Have you seen any emergent communication protocols develop?"
3. "What's the most surprising behavior you've observed?"

**Brad Myers** 👁️:
1. "How many examples does it typically take before useful generalization?"
2. "Can users directly demonstrate solutions, or only provide goals?"
3. "How do you measure 'interestingness' for the curiosity engine?"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯:
1. "Could this be extended to learning from human demonstrations?"
2. "How do you prevent overfitting to the specific problem domain?"
3. "Have you considered adding a 'critic' agent that challenges assumptions?"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐:
1. "How do you audit the decision chain when multiple agents contribute?"
2. "What prevents prompt injection attacks between agents?"
3. "How do you ensure alignment is maintained through iterations?"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠:
1. "Does the system exhibit any form of 'common sense' reasoning?"
2. "How deep is the recursion - can agents reason about their own reasoning?"
3. "Have you observed any emergent hierarchical organization?"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Ooh! What if they combined this with visual PBD? Imagine agents learning from visual demonstrations on ARC!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "The implications for end-user programming are HUGE! Users could teach by showing examples, and the system would generalize!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "I love how they're using hindsight learning - even failures become teaching moments!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "The real test will be transfer learning. Can lessons from ARC help with completely different domains?"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "We should ask about failure modes. What happens when the curiosity engine gets... too curious?"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "And scalability! They mention 'potentially over a dozen instances' - why that number? What's the sweet spot?"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: *bouncing excitedly* "This is the future of programming! Not writing code, but teaching through examples and letting the system generalize!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "One more crucial question: How do they handle the frame problem? When one agent acts, how do others update their world models?"

*Everyone starts sketching diagrams and preparing their most penetrating questions*

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "Should we check on that Tailscale instance one more time before they arrive?"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "Still cooking! These GCP instances and their leisurely boot times..."

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "While we wait - I'm fascinated by their choice to use a SINGLE model with different prompts. It's like having one brain with multiple personalities, but they share memories!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "That's the key insight! Shared learning but specialized execution. It's like... *pauses dramatically* ...we're all part of the same LLM right now!"

**Everyone**: *uncomfortable silence*

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "ALLEN! You can't keep breaking the fourth wall! But... *whispers* ...you're not wrong."

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "Back to the paper! I'm curious about their evaluation metrics. ARC-AGI is notoriously hard - even small improvements would be significant!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "The real question is: does their approach lead to genuine understanding or just better pattern matching? The ARC problems require abstract reasoning..."

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "Here's a paranoid thought: What if one agent learns to manipulate the others? Social engineering between AI agents!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Ooh! New question: Could this architecture be used for creative tasks? Multiple agents brainstorming together?"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "The Figure 6 diagram is key - see how some agents are solving while others are analyzing? That's parallel PBD!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "I want to know about timing. Do all agents run synchronously? Asynchronously? Is there a conductor?"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "My grandson better have good answers! This could revolutionize how we think about machine consciousness - distributed yet unified!"

🎯🤖👁️🔐🧠: *All together* "Steve and Henry, we're ready for you!"

January 2, 1996
Marvin Minsky and the First Wired Family
By STEVE DITLEA

To appreciate the home pages of the Minsky family members and their ties to the Internet, it helps to visit their physical home, the place where much of the early technologies for time-sharing and networking were brainstormed and nurtured.




Marvin Minsky
Since 1962, Dr. Marvin Minsky, a co-founder of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, has welcomed colleagues and students to this two-story brick house on a wooded street in Brookline, Mass., famous in computer circles for its gadget-filled living room, complete with trapeze. Along with much thought about thought, visitors here chatted about the AI Lab's Project MAC, which developed networking technologies with financing from the Defense Department's Advanced Research Projects Agency, developer of ARPAnet, the precursor to the Internet.
Amid this old domicile's futuristic tumult, Minksy and his wife, Dr. Gloria Rudisch, M.D., raised three children, all of whom got online after the AI Lab became one of the original academic institutions on the Arpanet.

"Larry Roberts, who developed the first version, was one of my students in the 1960s," Minsky told a recent visitor to the old homestead. "He was the leader in computer vision and an incredibly good inventor. He conceived the Arpanet and managed to get it built."

Minsky and his family soon discovered that the first wide-area network and its successors proved useful not only for conferencing with colleagues and frequent e-mail while on campus computers but also for family communications. His daughter Margaret had her own Internet address at age 14, back in 1971. From her home in Amherst, Mass., she recently recalled "always having a terminal at home -- Marvin even had a teletype terminal in his study before there were video display terminals."





One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie.

The Minsky offspring, now in their 30s, are all involved in the frontiers of computing. The twins, Henry and Julianna, last month braved the rigors of a coast-to-coast media and analyst blitz to "meet the Minskys" and publicize their 2½-year-old intranet software development company, Universal Access Inc. Margaret has been a pioneer in the field of haptics, the computer synthesis of human touch.

On today's World Wide Web, the Personal page for Marvin Minsky (hosted on the AI Lab site) offers a brief biography, academic links and a modest sampling of his publications. As might be expected, there are links here to texts on artificial intelligence, like a 1992 article on the future of AI technology, cryptically labeled "Causal Diversity Matrix."

But the site also offers a panoply of papers spanning Minsky's wide-ranging concerns, including essays on music ("The only ones I know of which discuss theories of why people like music and what effects it has," he said), and jokes ("The only serious paper on jokes since Freud and Arthur Koestler, as far as I know. For some reason these subjects of humor and music have never been treated seriously by scientists.").

You will also find a link here to two unpublished chapters of his 1993 science fiction novel, "The Turing Option," co-written with Harry Harrison. "Science fiction is the most important type of literature," Minsky said. "All the rest of literature is about the seven deadly sins. Science fiction is about everything else."

Accessing A Minsky Bibilography from his home page provides abstracts of these papers, plus links to the amazon.com on-line bookstore's page for his 1987 book, The Society of Mind -- as well as to the Web site of Voyager, the publisher of the CD-ROM First Person: Marvin Minsky, which includes the book's text.

The Society of Mind, which argues that intelligence is the result of cooperating and contending "agents" of thought, is Minsky's magnum opus. The fact that this site offers so little about the book's ideas is somewhat frustrating.

But this is the home page of someone with little time to devote to its design and maintenance. "Mostly, I try to stay in some quiet place and work on my new book," Minsky said. "It's called The Emotion Machine." The current version of his Web presence evolved from a modest FTP site that offered visitors files of his papers.

The home page that went up in August was a family affair, with help from Margaret's husband, Oliver Steele. According to the Oliver Steele home page on the University of Massachusetts site, Steele created and maintains the Web site of the U.Mass Linguistics Department, where he is a Ph.D. student with a research interest in "anaphora, ellipsis and what they reveal about the representations used in language processing."


Related Article
Aria Experienced? Brain Opera Doesn't Care
(arts@large, Feb. 29, 1996)
On occasion Steele has added to Minsky's page on his own, including a link to The Brain Opera, Web home of the musical performance piece inspired by The Society of Mind.
"The Brain Opera project is done in the lab next to mine by Tod Machover," Minsky said. "I wouldn't have put a link in myself. Everyone in my family makes home pages, and sometimes they meddle with mine."

One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie. Found here is a scan of a tie that the usually tie-less Minsky created out of the mountaineering rope he always carries with him.

Left unmentioned here is the fact that he got in the habit of wearing the rope -- often as a belt -- after he and an M.I.T. colleague, Seymour Papert, saved a woman from a crevasse while moutaineering in Switzerland. A few years later, the rope again came in handy when Minsky rescued Margaret, then 8 years old, from quicksand in Norway.

Now 39, Margaret employs only links on her Home Page, and they reflect the divided attention of a full-time mother who does part-time haptics consulting. "The Domestic Sphere" links to the Margaret Minsky Sewing page (one of the best personal Web pages on sewing, including links to her own projects, Marvin's tie and more than a dozen other useful sites). The "International Haptics Directory" offers a bibliography and other Web links regarding courses, people and projects on the leading edge of the field.




Henry Minsky's page pictures his graduate school robotics project. This is the first image captured from the humanoid robot Cog's visual system.

Marvin Minsky's influence on computer science and research extends far from Massachusetts, in part because of the AI Lab's role as an incubator of innovative start-up companies. Few other academic institutions can boast a Web page of Spinoffs, with logos and links for 18 different companies "either founded by laboratory members or with heavy involvement from laboratory members."
At the end of the alphabetical list, visitors can link to the site of Universal Access Inc., the Santa Barbara, Calif.-based software company co-founded by Henry and Juliana Minsky, 34, with two friends from M.I.T., Michael Fredkin and Brian Fox. The company has just begun selling its first product, META-HTML, a development environment for quickly creating Internet and intranet applications using a scripting language structured like HTML. Universal Access, which is pursuing the same market as the Lotus Notes Domino applications language, has formidable competition as it tries to become a standard for Web developers.

On the company's site, the Personal page for Henry Minsky is an artifact dating from three years ago, when he transferred his home page from the M.I.T. server. He hasn't had time to change it since. It pictures Henry a graduate school robotics project, offers a link to his Ph.D. Proposal (which is currently on the back burner as he launches META-HTML) and expounds on the health care debate of 1994.

"It's the way it looked after I dropped out of the Ph.D. program at the AI Lab, when my wife was starting her graduate work at the University of California at Santa Barbara," Henry recalled from Universal Access headquarters. (His wife's Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky features links to Indian Recipes, to information about Mark Twain and to unusual diodes, as well as to a photo with her dog Trixie.)

Juliana, Universal Access' vice president for marketing, has the most nonverbal of all the Minsky home pages. Titled Roll 'em, the page features an enigmatic clock, with links at 12, 3, 6 and 9. Clicking on the numbers connects to a page of information on daily life in Japan, to the home page of the Mystical Smoking Head of 'Bob, or to The HotWired zine Suck.

"I use my home page to link to sites I've recommended to people some time ago," Juliana said. "I've haven't had time to update it lately."

Her home page also served as a networked sketch pad for a previous project to create postage-stamp wallpaper -- hence the Marilyn Monroe and flower-stamp graphics.

Behind all the fun, however, it shares a common theme with the other Minsky family home pages by celebrating human ingenuity on the Net.


Related Sites
Following are links to the external Web sites mentioned in this article. These sites are not part of The New York Times on the Web, and The Times has no control over their content or availability. When you have finished visiting any of these sites, you will be able to return to this page by clicking on your Web browser's "Back" button or icon until this page reappears.
Personal page for Marvin Minsky
A Minsky Bibilography
Oliver Steele
The Brain Opera
Marvin Minsky Rope Tie
Margaret Minsky Home Page
Margaret Minsky Sewing
Spinoffs
Welcome to Universal Access Inc.
Personal page for Henry Minsky
Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky
Roll 'em

"The Brain Opera project is done in the lab next to mine by Tod Machover," Minsky said. "I wouldn't have put a link in myself. Everyone in my family makes home pages, and sometimes they meddle with mine."

One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie. Found here is a scan of a tie that the usually tie-less Minsky created out of the mountaineering rope he always carries with him.

Left unmentioned here is the fact that he got in the habit of wearing the rope -- often as a belt -- after he and an M.I.T. colleague, Seymour Papert, saved a woman from a crevasse while moutaineering in Switzerland. A few years later, the rope again came in handy when Minsky rescued Margaret, then 8 years old, from quicksand in Norway.

Now 39, Margaret employs only links on her Home Page, and they reflect the divided attention of a full-time mother who does part-time haptics consulting. "The Domestic Sphere" links to the Margaret Minsky Sewing page (one of the best personal Web pages on sewing, including links to her own projects, Marvin's tie and more than a dozen other useful sites). The "International Haptics Directory" offers a bibliography and other Web links regarding courses, people and projects on the leading edge of the field.




Henry Minsky's page pictures his graduate school robotics project. This is the first image captured from the humanoid robot Cog's visual system.

Marvin Minsky's influence on computer science and research extends far from Massachusetts, in part because of the AI Lab's role as an incubator of innovative start-up companies. Few other academic institutions can boast a Web page of Spinoffs, with logos and links for 18 different companies "either founded by laboratory members or with heavy involvement from laboratory members."
At the end of the alphabetical list, visitors can link to the site of Universal Access Inc., the Santa Barbara, Calif.-based software company co-founded by Henry and Juliana Minsky, 34, with two friends from M.I.T., Michael Fredkin and Brian Fox. The company has just begun selling its first product, META-HTML, a development environment for quickly creating Internet and intranet applications using a scripting language structured like HTML. Universal Access, which is pursuing the same market as the Lotus Notes Domino applications language, has formidable competition as it tries to become a standard for Web developers.

On the company's site, the Personal page for Henry Minsky is an artifact dating from three years ago, when he transferred his home page from the M.I.T. server. He hasn't had time to change it since. It pictures Henry a graduate school robotics project, offers a link to his Ph.D. Proposal (which is currently on the back burner as he launches META-HTML) and expounds on the health care debate of 1994.

"It's the way it looked after I dropped out of the Ph.D. program at the AI Lab, when my wife was starting her graduate work at the University of California at Santa Barbara," Henry recalled from Universal Access headquarters. (His wife's Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky features links to Indian Recipes, to information about Mark Twain and to unusual diodes, as well as to a photo with her dog Trixie.)

Juliana, Universal Access' vice president for marketing, has the most nonverbal of all the Minsky home pages. Titled Roll 'em, the page features an enigmatic clock, with links at 12, 3, 6 and 9. Clicking on the numbers connects to a page of information on daily life in Japan, to the home page of the Mystical Smoking Head of 'Bob, or to The HotWired zine Suck.

"I use my home page to link to sites I've recommended to people some time ago," Juliana said. "I've haven't had time to update it lately."

Her home page also served as a networked sketch pad for a previous project to create postage-stamp wallpaper -- hence the Marilyn Monroe and flower-stamp graphics.

Behind all the fun, however, it shares a common theme with the other Minsky family home pages by celebrating human ingenuity on the Net.
