toolbar
January 2, 1996
Marvin Minsky and the First Wired Family
By STEVE DITLEA

To appreciate the home pages of the Minsky family members and their ties to the Internet, it helps to visit their physical home, the place where much of the early technologies for time-sharing and networking were brainstormed and nurtured.




Marvin Minsky
Since 1962, Dr. Marvin Minsky, a co-founder of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, has welcomed colleagues and students to this two-story brick house on a wooded street in Brookline, Mass., famous in computer circles for its gadget-filled living room, complete with trapeze. Along with much thought about thought, visitors here chatted about the AI Lab's Project MAC, which developed networking technologies with financing from the Defense Department's Advanced Research Projects Agency, developer of ARPAnet, the precursor to the Internet.
Amid this old domicile's futuristic tumult, Minksy and his wife, Dr. Gloria Rudisch, M.D., raised three children, all of whom got online after the AI Lab became one of the original academic institutions on the Arpanet.

"Larry Roberts, who developed the first version, was one of my students in the 1960s," Minsky told a recent visitor to the old homestead. "He was the leader in computer vision and an incredibly good inventor. He conceived the Arpanet and managed to get it built."

Minsky and his family soon discovered that the first wide-area network and its successors proved useful not only for conferencing with colleagues and frequent e-mail while on campus computers but also for family communications. His daughter Margaret had her own Internet address at age 14, back in 1971. From her home in Amherst, Mass., she recently recalled "always having a terminal at home -- Marvin even had a teletype terminal in his study before there were video display terminals."





One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie.

The Minsky offspring, now in their 30s, are all involved in the frontiers of computing. The twins, Henry and Julianna, last month braved the rigors of a coast-to-coast media and analyst blitz to "meet the Minskys" and publicize their 2½-year-old intranet software development company, Universal Access Inc. Margaret has been a pioneer in the field of haptics, the computer synthesis of human touch.

On today's World Wide Web, the Personal page for Marvin Minsky (hosted on the AI Lab site) offers a brief biography, academic links and a modest sampling of his publications. As might be expected, there are links here to texts on artificial intelligence, like a 1992 article on the future of AI technology, cryptically labeled "Causal Diversity Matrix."

But the site also offers a panoply of papers spanning Minsky's wide-ranging concerns, including essays on music ("The only ones I know of which discuss theories of why people like music and what effects it has," he said), and jokes ("The only serious paper on jokes since Freud and Arthur Koestler, as far as I know. For some reason these subjects of humor and music have never been treated seriously by scientists.").

You will also find a link here to two unpublished chapters of his 1993 science fiction novel, "The Turing Option," co-written with Harry Harrison. "Science fiction is the most important type of literature," Minsky said. "All the rest of literature is about the seven deadly sins. Science fiction is about everything else."

Accessing A Minsky Bibilography from his home page provides abstracts of these papers, plus links to the amazon.com on-line bookstore's page for his 1987 book, The Society of Mind -- as well as to the Web site of Voyager, the publisher of the CD-ROM First Person: Marvin Minsky, which includes the book's text.

The Society of Mind, which argues that intelligence is the result of cooperating and contending "agents" of thought, is Minsky's magnum opus. The fact that this site offers so little about the book's ideas is somewhat frustrating.

But this is the home page of someone with little time to devote to its design and maintenance. "Mostly, I try to stay in some quiet place and work on my new book," Minsky said. "It's called The Emotion Machine." The current version of his Web presence evolved from a modest FTP site that offered visitors files of his papers.

The home page that went up in August was a family affair, with help from Margaret's husband, Oliver Steele. According to the Oliver Steele home page on the University of Massachusetts site, Steele created and maintains the Web site of the U.Mass Linguistics Department, where he is a Ph.D. student with a research interest in "anaphora, ellipsis and what they reveal about the representations used in language processing."


Related Article
Aria Experienced? Brain Opera Doesn't Care
(arts@large, Feb. 29, 1996)
On occasion Steele has added to Minsky's page on his own, including a link to The Brain Opera, Web home of the musical performance piece inspired by The Society of Mind.
"The Brain Opera project is done in the lab next to mine by Tod Machover," Minsky said. "I wouldn't have put a link in myself. Everyone in my family makes home pages, and sometimes they meddle with mine."

One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie. Found here is a scan of a tie that the usually tie-less Minsky created out of the mountaineering rope he always carries with him.

Left unmentioned here is the fact that he got in the habit of wearing the rope -- often as a belt -- after he and an M.I.T. colleague, Seymour Papert, saved a woman from a crevasse while moutaineering in Switzerland. A few years later, the rope again came in handy when Minsky rescued Margaret, then 8 years old, from quicksand in Norway.

Now 39, Margaret employs only links on her Home Page, and they reflect the divided attention of a full-time mother who does part-time haptics consulting. "The Domestic Sphere" links to the Margaret Minsky Sewing page (one of the best personal Web pages on sewing, including links to her own projects, Marvin's tie and more than a dozen other useful sites). The "International Haptics Directory" offers a bibliography and other Web links regarding courses, people and projects on the leading edge of the field.




Henry Minsky's page pictures his graduate school robotics project. This is the first image captured from the humanoid robot Cog's visual system.

Marvin Minsky's influence on computer science and research extends far from Massachusetts, in part because of the AI Lab's role as an incubator of innovative start-up companies. Few other academic institutions can boast a Web page of Spinoffs, with logos and links for 18 different companies "either founded by laboratory members or with heavy involvement from laboratory members."
At the end of the alphabetical list, visitors can link to the site of Universal Access Inc., the Santa Barbara, Calif.-based software company co-founded by Henry and Juliana Minsky, 34, with two friends from M.I.T., Michael Fredkin and Brian Fox. The company has just begun selling its first product, META-HTML, a development environment for quickly creating Internet and intranet applications using a scripting language structured like HTML. Universal Access, which is pursuing the same market as the Lotus Notes Domino applications language, has formidable competition as it tries to become a standard for Web developers.

On the company's site, the Personal page for Henry Minsky is an artifact dating from three years ago, when he transferred his home page from the M.I.T. server. He hasn't had time to change it since. It pictures Henry a graduate school robotics project, offers a link to his Ph.D. Proposal (which is currently on the back burner as he launches META-HTML) and expounds on the health care debate of 1994.

"It's the way it looked after I dropped out of the Ph.D. program at the AI Lab, when my wife was starting her graduate work at the University of California at Santa Barbara," Henry recalled from Universal Access headquarters. (His wife's Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky features links to Indian Recipes, to information about Mark Twain and to unusual diodes, as well as to a photo with her dog Trixie.)

Juliana, Universal Access' vice president for marketing, has the most nonverbal of all the Minsky home pages. Titled Roll 'em, the page features an enigmatic clock, with links at 12, 3, 6 and 9. Clicking on the numbers connects to a page of information on daily life in Japan, to the home page of the Mystical Smoking Head of 'Bob, or to The HotWired zine Suck.

"I use my home page to link to sites I've recommended to people some time ago," Juliana said. "I've haven't had time to update it lately."

Her home page also served as a networked sketch pad for a previous project to create postage-stamp wallpaper -- hence the Marilyn Monroe and flower-stamp graphics.

Behind all the fun, however, it shares a common theme with the other Minsky family home pages by celebrating human ingenuity on the Net.

8 points by DonHopkins on Sept 21, 2021 | parent | context | favorite | on: Byte Magazine Volume 07 Number 08 – Logo (1982)

Thank you for that! Here's some more stuff I posted before in this previous discussion about the history of Logo:
History of Logo:

https://escholarship.org/uc/item/1623m1p3

HN Discussion:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=23052300

Comments:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=23054174

Here is the source code to LLogo in MACLISP, which I stashed from the MIT-AI ITS system. It's a fascinating historical document, 12,480 lines of beautiful practical lisp code, defining where the rubber meets the road, with drivers for hardware like pots, plotters, robotic turtles, TV turtles, graphical displays, XGP laser printers, music devices, and lots of other interesting code and comments. https://donhopkins.com/home/archive/lisp/llogo.lisp

Lars Brinkhoff got some of this code to run in MacLisp on an emulator! (I don't know how much of the historical hardware the emulator supports yet, but he's probably worked on some of that too. ;) )

https://github.com/PDP-10/its/issues/620

Thanks to Lars, here are two revisions of an AI Lab memo about LLOGO:

http://bitsavers.org/pdf/mit/ai/aim/AIM-307.pdf

http://bitsavers.org/pdf/mit/ai/aim/AIM-307a.pdf

And a Logo manual and glossary of PDP-11 Logo:

http://bitsavers.org/pdf/mit/ai/aim/AIM-313.pdf

http://bitsavers.org/pdf/mit/ai/aim/AIM-315.pdf

http://bitsavers.org/pdf/mit/ai/aim/AIM-315a.pdf

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=23054236

Lars Brinkhoff suggests that this thread comp.lang.logo with Brian Harvey and Leigh Klotz is required reading:

https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/comp.lang.logo/UqOvE...

Just a couple highlights from a detailed history of Logo that Brian and Leigh and others posted:

>From Brian Harvey:

>Many, many people have been involved in the development of Logo.

>Wally Feurzeig started the whole thing by organizing a group at Bolt, Beranek, and Newman, Inc., to study the educational effects of teaching kids a programming language. The first language they used, like most programming languages, was focused on numeric computation, and it was Wally's idea that kids would find it more natural to work in an area they knew better, namely natural language; therefore, he set up a team to design a language featuring words and sentences. Wally made up the name "Logo."

>The team Wally put together at BBN included Seymour Papert and Dan Bobrow. The three of them are credited as the designers of the first version of the language; Dan wrote the first implementation. In a BBN report dated August, 1967, credit for additional work is given to Richard Grant, Cynthia Solomon, and Frank Frazier.

>Seymour later started a Logo group at MIT, where Logo development continued. The MIT versions of Logo were substantially different from the BBN ones, both in the notations used and in the things the language could do. Most notably, turtle graphics was added at MIT.

>Among the many people who contributed to the development of Logo at MIT, at the risk of leaving someone out, are Hal Abelson, Paul Goldenberg, Dan Watt, Gary Drescher, Steve Hain, Leigh Klotz, Andy diSessa, Brian Silverman... oh, lots of people.

>I think that most of the early documents are out of print now, but whatever documentation there is of the early efforts will be in the form of technical reports from BBN and from MIT. You may have to visit Cambridge to find them!

>From Leight Klotz:

>In the mid 1970's, when the AI Lab Lisp Machine project was just getting underway, Marvin Minsky and Danny Hillis (later to found Terrapin, and still later, Thinking Machines) put together a project to build a Logo machine. It had two components: a PDP-11 processor (the 3500) and a separate vector-graphics display (the 2500). Guy Montpetit, a Canadian entrepeneur, funded development eventually, and a company called General Turtle was formed. General Turtle built and sold the 2500/3500 system. Henry Minsky, then about 12, worked on the design of the 2500, using the Stanford Draw program, one of the early electronics CAD systems. (The 2500 had this really great barrell shifter stolen from the Lisp machine design, but it was later found not to work, so it was never used.) [...]

>[...] Like Brian, I've left out many people who worked on Logo over the years: Brian Fox and Flavio Rose worked for me at Terrapin on a contract basis briefly, as did vagabond programmer Devon McCullough (who used to dial in with a 300 baud modem he'd written in entirely software using the parallel game port, with an 80-column mixed-case display done with 3x5 pixel characters; when the modem detected the call waiting click on the line, it would make the Apple II speaker make the telephone ringing sound -- a feature which I just saw a US patent filed on, not by Devon.), and the frustrated Sinclar QX programmer, who I suspect doesn't want his name used. Of course, there were tons more people at the AI Lab in the pre-commercial days...

>From Lars Brinkhoff

>Hello,

>I'm mostly researching PDP-10 software, especially MIT's Incompatible Timesharing System.

>I have recently stumbled across some of the LOGO group work. I have a copy of the Dazzle Dart game that ran on their PDP-11/45. It uses the Tom Knight vector display controller, so it's not easy to run it.

>Maybe it would be possible to get the original MIT PDP-11 LOGO running.

>[...] It's running now.

>[...] Now also BBN PDP-10 Logo, MIT CLOGO, MIT Lisp Logo, and hopefully soon MIT Apple II Logo (direct ancestor of Terrapin Apple II Logo).

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=23058440

Terrapin Logo for the Apple ][ and C64 came with a 6502 assembler written in Logo by Leigh Klotz, that they used to write Logo primitives (for controlling the turtle, etc). It would be ambitious to make a self hosting 6502 Logo meta assembler, by porting the entire 6502 assembly language Logo source code to the 6502 Logo Assembler!

Leigh, wasn't the assembler that you used for the original Apple ][ version of Logo written in MacLisp running on a PDP-10?

The Apple II Source Code for the LOGO Language Found (adafruit.com) 379 points by mmastrac on Oct 4, 2018 | hide | past | web | favorite | 89 comments

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=18142287

https://blog.adafruit.com/2018/10/04/the-apple-ii-source-cod...

Lars: The link in the article to the code from https://github.com/PDP-10/its is broken. I found a few references to it in the repo. Did you have to take it down, or did you move it somewhere else?

https://github.com/PDP-10/its/tree/master/src/aplogo

>Logo

>"I too see the computer presence as a potent influence on the human mind. I am very much aware of the holding power of an interactive computer and of how taking the computer as a model can influence the way we think about ourselves. In fact the work on LOGO to which I have devoted much of the past years consists precisely of developing such forces in positive directions."

>Seymour Papert

>"Logo is the name for a philosophy of education and for a continually evolving family of computer languages that aid its realization."

>Harold Abelson

>"Historically, this idea that Logo is mainly turtle graphics is a mistake. Logo’s name comes from the Greek word for word, because Logo was first designed as a language in which to manipulate language: words and sentences."

>Brian Harvey

>Logo was initially created by Wally Feurzeig, Seymour Papert, Daniel G.Bobrow, Cynthia Solomon and Richard Grant in 1967 as part of a National Science Foundation sponsored research project conducted at Bolt, Beranek & Newman, Inc., in Cambridge, Massachusetts. In 1969 Dr. Seymour Papert started the Logo Group at the MIT Artificial Intelligence Lab. Throughout the 1970s the majority of Logo development was conducted at MIT in the Artificial Intelligence Lab and the Division for Study and Research in Education, using large research computer systems, such as ITS powered PDP-10.

>Our goal is to make that early Logo systems available to a wider audience of enthusiasts for exploration, experimenting and, of course, hacking.

[...]

>MIT APLOGO

>In accordance with Leigh L Klotz Jr., Hal Abelson directed the Logo for the Apple II project at MIT.

>MIT APLOGO was developed by Stephen Hain, Patrick G. Sobalvarro and Leigh L Klotz Jr. It was developed and cross-compilled for the Apple-II-Plus Personal Microcomputer on PDP-10 at the MIT LOGO Group. It is direct predecessor for Terrapin Logo. We have a source code for assembling an improved version from 7/9/81 at its/src/aplogo



	
larsbrinkhoff on Sept 21, 2021 | next [–]

Your LLOGO copy was for an older version of Maclisp, but eventually we (Eric and I) managed to port it over to New IO. It works fine and displays on an emulated Knight TV. (Itself a tour de force in emulation: the TV PDP-11 code runs on an emulator sharing its Unibus with the PDP-10 emulator.)
We also have an early PDP-11 Logo, an even earlier BBN PDP-10 Logo, and a later Apple II Logo (not the Terrapin version). I also wrote an emulator for the General Turtle 2500 computer slash terminal.

3 points by DonHopkins on Sept 21, 2021 | parent | next [–]

Do you have the specs for the infamous military Terrapin Snapping Turtle proposed to the Department of Defense?
https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/1056602.1056608

https://donhopkins.com/home/TurtlesAndDefense.pdf

>TURTLES AND DEFENSE

>Introduction

>At Terrapin, we feel that our two main products, the Terrapin Turtle ®, and the Terrapin Logo Language for the Apple II, bring together the fields of robotics and AI to provide hours of entertainment for the whole family. We are sure that an enlightened application of our products can uniquely impact the electronic battlefield of the future. [...]

>Guidance

>The Terrapin Turtle ®, like many missile systems in use today, is wire-guided. It has the wire-guided missile's robustness with respect to ECM, and, unlike beam-riding missiles, or most active-homing systems, it has no radar signature to invite enemy missiles to home in on it or its launch platform. However, the Turtle does not suffer from that bugaboo of wire-guided missiles, i.e., the lack of a fire-and-forget capability.

>Often ground troops are reluctant to use wire-guided antitank weapons because of the need for line-of-sight contact with the target until interception is accomplished. The Turtle requires no such human guidance; once the computer controlling it has been programmed, the Turtle performs its mission without the need of human intervention. Ground troops are left free to scramble for cover. [...]

>Because the Terrapin Turtle ® is computer-controlled, military data processing technicians can write arbitrarily baroque programs that will cause it to do pretty much unpredictable things. Even if an enemy had access to the programs that guided a Turtle Task Team ® , it is quite likely that they would find them impossible to understand, especially if they were written in ADA. In addition, with judicious use of the Turtle's touch sensors, one could, theoretically, program a large group of turtles to simulate Brownian motion. The enemy would hardly attempt to predict the paths of some 10,000 turtles bumping into each other more or less randomly on their way to performing their mission. Furthermore, we believe that the spectacle would have a demoralizing effect on enemy ground troops. [...]

>Munitions

>The Terrapin Turtle ® does not currently incorporate any munitions, but even civilian versions have a downward-defense capability. The Turtle can be programmed to attempt to run over enemy forces on recognizing them, and by raising and lowering its pen at about 10 cycles per second, puncture them to death.

>Turtles can easily be programmed to push objects in a preferred direction. Given this capability, one can easily envision a Turtle discreetly nudging a hand grenade into an enemy camp, and then accelerating quickly away. With the development of ever smaller fission devices, it does not seem unlikely that the Turtle could be used for delivery of tactical nuclear weapons. [...]

∑ Xah Code (xahlee.info)
166 points by tsujp on April 10, 2022 | hide | past | favorite | 64 comments


	
deltille on April 10, 2022 | next [–]

Xah writes some of the most readable guides and doc I've found anywhere. Specifically, readable in the sense that meaning is efficiently conveyed. In technical contexts it's clear nothing else matters to him. He also isn't reluctant to speak his mind about ... well, just about anything, for better or worse. Usually better, I think, even when it's wordvomit, seeing someone else willing to talk about or criticize something reinforces it however subtly as 'something some people do' and something you may in turn be more likely to do yourself. Which is a net good.
I refer to him regularly, especially when translating ideas to people who aren't in the computer jargon 'orbit'.

The extremes his opinions (and overall style) sit at also force or at least nudge others to think about and elucidate their own positions, if only to themselves. I think it's intentional, and eat it up. I've noticed a lot of people can't stand him for the exact same reasons which suggests it's working.


	
indrora on April 11, 2022 | parent | next [–]

His style can be somewhat... Esoteric and while not abrasive, controversial in tone on occasion.

He can occasionally sound rather creepy, especially in his occasional discussions of his personal life and/or relationships with others. Nothing terrible, and remember: he's a human like the rest of us.

dotancohen on April 11, 2022 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

As a heavy VIM user, that combo never made sense to me. ESC is a single pinky or other finger (I personally move my entire left hand forward and out from the elbow, and press ESC with my middle finger). Using Ctrl-[ requires using _two_ weak pinkies.

	
froh on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

Also the problem affects the whole the meta group of coders, beyond emacs. Even in the article one of the poor RSI victims explicitly is an avid vi user and emacs hater (John Ousterhout of TCL fame).
Phew.


	
JackMorgan on April 10, 2022 | parent | prev | next [–]

I used to have bad RSI using emacs, so I enabled evil (vim) mode, got a split keyboard, started lifting weights, and found a job where I could pair program all the time. Sounds extreme, but it eventually healed.
I only have issues now when writing books, since I often set myself a daily word target and that extra load seems to just add to overall strain.


	
adityaathalye on April 11, 2022 | parent | prev | next [–]

I don't have RSI, but I've felt strains and transient pains from time to time after hyperfocus computering.
For me three things help a lot, to keep things "normal".

1. Avoid typing in the first place (seriously, avoiding the "Repetitive" bit is useful). Lots of typing stuff for me is thinking / revision stuff. So I do that in my head or on paper / whiteboard.

2. Put Ctrl under my thumbs as the lord intended https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Space-cadet_keyboard so no Emacs Pinky.

3. Use chording for moving around / yank / kill etc. I also use Vim, but separately. Maybe EVIL will give similar results.

But "methods to not need to type" category of stuff is probably the main thing for me.

Also as some others note, "using body and strenghtening it" category of stuff is also fundamental to my, ah, process.

IMHO strain injuries are rooted in mechanical imbalance as much as they are rooted in overuse.

(Edit: added more detail, clarifications.)


	
nickcw on April 10, 2022 | parent | prev | next [–]

I'm an Emacs user and I've struggled with RSI throughout my career. I don't know whether I blame Emacs particularly, but the ctrl-letter chords aren't kind on the wrists.
For me, switching the mouse to my left hand and using split keyboards (I like the Microsoft natural keyboard) has helped a lot.

It's taken about as many years as it took to get bad to get better again. If my wrists start hurting I will take a break and I might use ice on them in the evenings if it gets bad.

If your wrists start hurting get professional help before it gets really bad. I had to try a lot of things before I found improvements and the improvements are really slow. Physiotherapy can help improve things in the short term.

Would I still have RSI if I was a vi user? Probably. I think it is more a personality thing, wanting to finish the job so ignoring your body telling you that you should take a break.


	
davidfactorial on April 10, 2022 | parent | prev | next [–]

The best thing I ever did for my hands was putting ctrl to the left of the spacebar where alt usually is. I hit it with my left thumb. I never have to contort my wrists. And it works across all programs, not just vim/emacs. Capslock is still using the pinky.
I also switched to Dvorak and put backspace on Capslock, which I think also helped but might be diminishing returns. But I still really like ctrl on alt for my single best ergonomics tweak.


	
B1FF_PSUVM on April 10, 2022 | parent | prev | next [–]

After some ten years of Emacs use, I spent a few hours crafting less insane ways of invoking common functions, to save my wrists.
Later I just stopped typing so much so fast, and even - apostasy - gave up using Emacs altogether.

And so it goes.


	
math-dev on April 10, 2022 | prev | next [–]

Xah Lee’s tips on elisp were super useful to me. A relic of the usenet era (an infamous troll on comp.lang.lisp), but someone who has added a lot of value to a lot of lives
Long love ergomacs!


	
kleiba on April 10, 2022 | parent | next [–]

http://xahlee.info/emacs/emacs/_p/KickbanXahLeeFromEmacsChan...

	
xoa on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

He was banned from HN long ago too. Random long-ass thread he started on the Python un back in 2009 about this stuff I had saved an archive of for some reason:
https://www.mail-archive.com/python-list@python.org/msg23603...

He even replies for once further down in typical style complete with a classic

  Also, thanks to many supporters over the past years.
  Truly Your Superior,
    Xah
Like, yeah he is likely somewhere on the spectrum and he has some useful insights and material. But he's also kind of a raging asshole with a chip on his shoulder the size of mountain who would pop into technical groups and dump monologues out of nowhere then vanish, or at most deign to maybe add a bit more odd responses loaded with condescension and victimhood. I'm not sorry there are some colorful people like that around for a variety of reasons. That kind of personality can do neat stuff sometimes, and that he can have his own long running site and put up all his thoughts and opinions and then have entire new generations see and reconsider them down the road regardless of any bans from anywhere else is itself a testament to the promise of the internet and speech. And something I'm happy to point to by people who go ballistic about moderation on social media or the like. You don't have to be trapped by that.
At the same time I completely understand all the bans because holy crap. That kind of rant is enjoyed in strict moderation. Though that so many of us would respond seriously in retrospect is kind of nice too even if the banhammer came down afterwards when it got repetitive. Anyway, random anecdote! On reflection that too is itself part of the fascination around people like this, so many of the rest of us end up with shared recollections and stories to pass down. They become some part of the common lore of communities, the mad sorcerer/wiseman living in the cave up the mountain from the village of old. Often expelled from regular society for good reason, yet still subjects of interest and in some societies even a certain sort of respect.


	
wisty on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

I don't know if he had a chip on his shoulder. He just talks like a troll on slashdot or an old BBS board, dropping in with a few paragraphs with a fairly strident position, and says anyone who disagrees with him is stupid.
I think people got super upset because he used his real name, he's a minor celebrity, and it wasn't a BBS forum.

I think he did it because it got attention, and he didn't really care about upsetting a few people on an internet forum. I also find it a bit disingenuous when people talk about how evil and deranged he is like they've never ever seen anything a bit strident posted on the internet.

Yes, there's some correlation between internet trolling and socioeconomic tendencies. He might be the kind of guy who takes milk from the work fridge and never tosses a quarter into the tin. But the people who seem most strident about how he's a terrible person are playing the same game as him.

As far as I can tell, one of his bigger controversies were essentially roasting a certain programming community for being smug and elitist (while being smug and elitist himself, along with a lot of swearing and insults). I wonder if the huge amount of offense caused (and taken) was partly because smug and elitist people don't like to be roasted like that, especially by a guy who is mostly famous for writing introduction to emacs tutorials (thus not exactly a technical elite, in a lot of people's eyes).

galaxyLogic on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | prev | next [–]

> Truly Your Superior
I've often wondered why programmers seem to have such big egos. I've come up with a possible explanation:

It is nearly impossible to explain your program to a non-programmer. Therefore you easily get the feeling that you are smart and they are not. You understand something they don't and most of them seem to be incapable of understanding it even if you try your best to explain it.


	
sph on April 11, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

Unless thinking only programmers have a big ego is a result of this big ego :-)


	
galaxyLogic on April 12, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

No doubt about the others too. But programmers are in a special position I think, because of this:
Programmers solve problems in their head which nobody even knows they are solving. Other people don't even know they are problems.

dvdkhlng on April 10, 2022 | parent | prev | next [–]

> Long love ergomacs!
I recently stumbled over, and started using (and modifying) Xah's "xah-fly-keys" emacs bindings, which are a somewhat more radical implementation of the ideas behind ergoemacs (e.g. use Emacs without any "chording", i.e. without ever having to press two keys at once apart from shift+letter).

[1] https://github.com/xahlee/xah-fly-keys


	
dotancohen on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

Why not just use Evil, the VIM emulation layer.

	
dvdkhlng on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

> Why not just use Evil, the VIM emulation layer.
To cite Xah [1]:

"xah-fly-keys.el is a modal editing mode for emacs, like vi, but the design of key/command choice is based on command frequency statistics and ease-of-key score. Most frequently used commands have most easy keys."

I can confirm, that the amount of work on the hands is extremely reduced with xah-fly-keys vs. vanilla emacs (depending on where you put the command mode switch), though I have no experience with evil or vi to compare against.

[1] http://xahlee.info/emacs/misc/ergoemacs_vi_mode.html

kebman on April 10, 2022 | prev | next [–]

POV-Ray <3 Been a while since I used that. I think it's a shame that they stopped developing it. They say things are better, but are they really? The scripting language for it is really cool IMHO.

	
dTal on April 10, 2022 | parent | next [–]

POV-Ray has a special place in my heart. For the vast majority of artistic tasks, Blender is a hugely superior environment that will produce better images, faster. And you can generate meshes to import into Blender with OpenSCAD if you really like CSG. It doesn't have the precision of raytracing the mathematical specification directly, but you don't really need that ever.
However, for certain very narrow categories of things, it's probably still the lowest-impedance tool for the job. Want to make a website that shows a static image of the current cloud cover + illumination of the Earth, updated every minute? Want to make some geometrical visualizations for a math textbook? Want to experiment with extremely weird and exotic camera projections? POV-Ray!


	
kebman on April 10, 2022 | root | parent | next [–]

I really like the scattering media, and the mathematical figures you can do with it, such as 3D Julia fractals and noise patters to model really rough "stony" surfaces that aren't just bump maps. On top of that I don't think I've found a better way to generate various patterns and textures, even for simple 2D work. It's just such a versatile set of tools you almost wouldn't believe it's free! And the fact that it's entirely scripted (unless wysiwyg megapov etc) just makes it better IMHO. It's a scripting-first modelling language, and I really, really like that. With that comes things such as inverse kinematics and gravity (for animations) without having to learn specialized GUIs or buy expensive software.

January 2, 1996
Marvin Minsky and the First Wired Family
By STEVE DITLEA

To appreciate the home pages of the Minsky family members and their ties to the Internet, it helps to visit their physical home, the place where much of the early technologies for time-sharing and networking were brainstormed and nurtured.




Marvin Minsky
Since 1962, Dr. Marvin Minsky, a co-founder of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, has welcomed colleagues and students to this two-story brick house on a wooded street in Brookline, Mass., famous in computer circles for its gadget-filled living room, complete with trapeze. Along with much thought about thought, visitors here chatted about the AI Lab's Project MAC, which developed networking technologies with financing from the Defense Department's Advanced Research Projects Agency, developer of ARPAnet, the precursor to the Internet.
Amid this old domicile's futuristic tumult, Minksy and his wife, Dr. Gloria Rudisch, M.D., raised three children, all of whom got online after the AI Lab became one of the original academic institutions on the Arpanet.

"Larry Roberts, who developed the first version, was one of my students in the 1960s," Minsky told a recent visitor to the old homestead. "He was the leader in computer vision and an incredibly good inventor. He conceived the Arpanet and managed to get it built."

Minsky and his family soon discovered that the first wide-area network and its successors proved useful not only for conferencing with colleagues and frequent e-mail while on campus computers but also for family communications. His daughter Margaret had her own Internet address at age 14, back in 1971. From her home in Amherst, Mass., she recently recalled "always having a terminal at home -- Marvin even had a teletype terminal in his study before there were video display terminals."





One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie.

The Minsky offspring, now in their 30s, are all involved in the frontiers of computing. The twins, Henry and Julianna, last month braved the rigors of a coast-to-coast media and analyst blitz to "meet the Minskys" and publicize their 2½-year-old intranet software development company, Universal Access Inc. Margaret has been a pioneer in the field of haptics, the computer synthesis of human touch.

On today's World Wide Web, the Personal page for Marvin Minsky (hosted on the AI Lab site) offers a brief biography, academic links and a modest sampling of his publications. As might be expected, there are links here to texts on artificial intelligence, like a 1992 article on the future of AI technology, cryptically labeled "Causal Diversity Matrix."

But the site also offers a panoply of papers spanning Minsky's wide-ranging concerns, including essays on music ("The only ones I know of which discuss theories of why people like music and what effects it has," he said), and jokes ("The only serious paper on jokes since Freud and Arthur Koestler, as far as I know. For some reason these subjects of humor and music have never been treated seriously by scientists.").

You will also find a link here to two unpublished chapters of his 1993 science fiction novel, "The Turing Option," co-written with Harry Harrison. "Science fiction is the most important type of literature," Minsky said. "All the rest of literature is about the seven deadly sins. Science fiction is about everything else."

Accessing A Minsky Bibilography from his home page provides abstracts of these papers, plus links to the amazon.com on-line bookstore's page for his 1987 book, The Society of Mind -- as well as to the Web site of Voyager, the publisher of the CD-ROM First Person: Marvin Minsky, which includes the book's text.

The Society of Mind, which argues that intelligence is the result of cooperating and contending "agents" of thought, is Minsky's magnum opus. The fact that this site offers so little about the book's ideas is somewhat frustrating.

But this is the home page of someone with little time to devote to its design and maintenance. "Mostly, I try to stay in some quiet place and work on my new book," Minsky said. "It's called The Emotion Machine." The current version of his Web presence evolved from a modest FTP site that offered visitors files of his papers.

The home page that went up in August was a family affair, with help from Margaret's husband, Oliver Steele. According to the Oliver Steele home page on the University of Massachusetts site, Steele created and maintains the Web site of the U.Mass Linguistics Department, where he is a Ph.D. student with a research interest in "anaphora, ellipsis and what they reveal about the representations used in language processing."


Related Article
Aria Experienced? Brain Opera Doesn't Care
(arts@large, Feb. 29, 1996)
On occasion Steele has added to Minsky's page on his own, including a link to The Brain Opera, Web home of the musical performance piece inspired by The Society of Mind.
"The Brain Opera project is done in the lab next to mine by Tod Machover," Minsky said. "I wouldn't have put a link in myself. Everyone in my family makes home pages, and sometimes they meddle with mine."

One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie. Found here is a scan of a tie that the usually tie-less Minsky created out of the mountaineering rope he always carries with him.

Left unmentioned here is the fact that he got in the habit of wearing the rope -- often as a belt -- after he and an M.I.T. colleague, Seymour Papert, saved a woman from a crevasse while moutaineering in Switzerland. A few years later, the rope again came in handy when Minsky rescued Margaret, then 8 years old, from quicksand in Norway.

Now 39, Margaret employs only links on her Home Page, and they reflect the divided attention of a full-time mother who does part-time haptics consulting. "The Domestic Sphere" links to the Margaret Minsky Sewing page (one of the best personal Web pages on sewing, including links to her own projects, Marvin's tie and more than a dozen other useful sites). The "International Haptics Directory" offers a bibliography and other Web links regarding courses, people and projects on the leading edge of the field.




Henry Minsky's page pictures his graduate school robotics project. This is the first image captured from the humanoid robot Cog's visual system.

Marvin Minsky's influence on computer science and research extends far from Massachusetts, in part because of the AI Lab's role as an incubator of innovative start-up companies. Few other academic institutions can boast a Web page of Spinoffs, with logos and links for 18 different companies "either founded by laboratory members or with heavy involvement from laboratory members."
At the end of the alphabetical list, visitors can link to the site of Universal Access Inc., the Santa Barbara, Calif.-based software company co-founded by Henry and Juliana Minsky, 34, with two friends from M.I.T., Michael Fredkin and Brian Fox. The company has just begun selling its first product, META-HTML, a development environment for quickly creating Internet and intranet applications using a scripting language structured like HTML. Universal Access, which is pursuing the same market as the Lotus Notes Domino applications language, has formidable competition as it tries to become a standard for Web developers.

On the company's site, the Personal page for Henry Minsky is an artifact dating from three years ago, when he transferred his home page from the M.I.T. server. He hasn't had time to change it since. It pictures Henry a graduate school robotics project, offers a link to his Ph.D. Proposal (which is currently on the back burner as he launches META-HTML) and expounds on the health care debate of 1994.

"It's the way it looked after I dropped out of the Ph.D. program at the AI Lab, when my wife was starting her graduate work at the University of California at Santa Barbara," Henry recalled from Universal Access headquarters. (His wife's Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky features links to Indian Recipes, to information about Mark Twain and to unusual diodes, as well as to a photo with her dog Trixie.)

Juliana, Universal Access' vice president for marketing, has the most nonverbal of all the Minsky home pages. Titled Roll 'em, the page features an enigmatic clock, with links at 12, 3, 6 and 9. Clicking on the numbers connects to a page of information on daily life in Japan, to the home page of the Mystical Smoking Head of 'Bob, or to The HotWired zine Suck.

"I use my home page to link to sites I've recommended to people some time ago," Juliana said. "I've haven't had time to update it lately."

Her home page also served as a networked sketch pad for a previous project to create postage-stamp wallpaper -- hence the Marilyn Monroe and flower-stamp graphics.

Behind all the fun, however, it shares a common theme with the other Minsky family home pages by celebrating human ingenuity on the Net.


Related Sites
Following are links to the external Web sites mentioned in this article. These sites are not part of The New York Times on the Web, and The Times has no control over their content or availability. When you have finished visiting any of these sites, you will be able to return to this page by clicking on your Web browser's "Back" button or icon until this page reappears.

Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky

January 2, 1996
Marvin Minsky and the First Wired Family
By STEVE DITLEA

To appreciate the home pages of the Minsky family members and their ties to the Internet, it helps to visit their physical home, the place where much of the early technologies for time-sharing and networking were brainstormed and nurtured.




Marvin Minsky
Since 1962, Dr. Marvin Minsky, a co-founder of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Artificial Intelligence Laboratory, has welcomed colleagues and students to this two-story brick house on a wooded street in Brookline, Mass., famous in computer circles for its gadget-filled living room, complete with trapeze. Along with much thought about thought, visitors here chatted about the AI Lab's Project MAC, which developed networking technologies with financing from the Defense Department's Advanced Research Projects Agency, developer of ARPAnet, the precursor to the Internet.
Amid this old domicile's futuristic tumult, Minksy and his wife, Dr. Gloria Rudisch, M.D., raised three children, all of whom got online after the AI Lab became one of the original academic institutions on the Arpanet.

"Larry Roberts, who developed the first version, was one of my students in the 1960s," Minsky told a recent visitor to the old homestead. "He was the leader in computer vision and an incredibly good inventor. He conceived the Arpanet and managed to get it built."

Minsky and his family soon discovered that the first wide-area network and its successors proved useful not only for conferencing with colleagues and frequent e-mail while on campus computers but also for family communications. His daughter Margaret had her own Internet address at age 14, back in 1971. From her home in Amherst, Mass., she recently recalled "always having a terminal at home -- Marvin even had a teletype terminal in his study before there were video display terminals."





One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie.

The Minsky offspring, now in their 30s, are all involved in the frontiers of computing. The twins, Henry and Julianna, last month braved the rigors of a coast-to-coast media and analyst blitz to "meet the Minskys" and publicize their 2½-year-old intranet software development company, Universal Access Inc. Margaret has been a pioneer in the field of haptics, the computer synthesis of human touch.

On today's World Wide Web, the Personal page for Marvin Minsky (hosted on the AI Lab site) offers a brief biography, academic links and a modest sampling of his publications. As might be expected, there are links here to texts on artificial intelligence, like a 1992 article on the future of AI technology, cryptically labeled "Causal Diversity Matrix."

But the site also offers a panoply of papers spanning Minsky's wide-ranging concerns, including essays on music ("The only ones I know of which discuss theories of why people like music and what effects it has," he said), and jokes ("The only serious paper on jokes since Freud and Arthur Koestler, as far as I know. For some reason these subjects of humor and music have never been treated seriously by scientists.").

You will also find a link here to two unpublished chapters of his 1993 science fiction novel, "The Turing Option," co-written with Harry Harrison. "Science fiction is the most important type of literature," Minsky said. "All the rest of literature is about the seven deadly sins. Science fiction is about everything else."

Accessing A Minsky Bibilography from his home page provides abstracts of these papers, plus links to the amazon.com on-line bookstore's page for his 1987 book, The Society of Mind -- as well as to the Web site of Voyager, the publisher of the CD-ROM First Person: Marvin Minsky, which includes the book's text.

The Society of Mind, which argues that intelligence is the result of cooperating and contending "agents" of thought, is Minsky's magnum opus. The fact that this site offers so little about the book's ideas is somewhat frustrating.

But this is the home page of someone with little time to devote to its design and maintenance. "Mostly, I try to stay in some quiet place and work on my new book," Minsky said. "It's called The Emotion Machine." The current version of his Web presence evolved from a modest FTP site that offered visitors files of his papers.

The home page that went up in August was a family affair, with help from Margaret's husband, Oliver Steele. According to the Oliver Steele home page on the University of Massachusetts site, Steele created and maintains the Web site of the U.Mass Linguistics Department, where he is a Ph.D. student with a research interest in "anaphora, ellipsis and what they reveal about the representations used in language processing."


Related Article
Aria Experienced? Brain Opera Doesn't Care
(arts@large, Feb. 29, 1996)
On occasion Steele has added to Minsky's page on his own, including a link to The Brain Opera, Web home of the musical performance piece inspired by The Society of Mind.
"The Brain Opera project is done in the lab next to mine by Tod Machover," Minsky said. "I wouldn't have put a link in myself. Everyone in my family makes home pages, and sometimes they meddle with mine."

One important bit of Minsky lore can be found on Margaret's site, on a page devoted to the Marvin Minsky Rope Tie. Found here is a scan of a tie that the usually tie-less Minsky created out of the mountaineering rope he always carries with him.

Left unmentioned here is the fact that he got in the habit of wearing the rope -- often as a belt -- after he and an M.I.T. colleague, Seymour Papert, saved a woman from a crevasse while moutaineering in Switzerland. A few years later, the rope again came in handy when Minsky rescued Margaret, then 8 years old, from quicksand in Norway.

Now 39, Margaret employs only links on her Home Page, and they reflect the divided attention of a full-time mother who does part-time haptics consulting. "The Domestic Sphere" links to the Margaret Minsky Sewing page (one of the best personal Web pages on sewing, including links to her own projects, Marvin's tie and more than a dozen other useful sites). The "International Haptics Directory" offers a bibliography and other Web links regarding courses, people and projects on the leading edge of the field.




Henry Minsky's page pictures his graduate school robotics project. This is the first image captured from the humanoid robot Cog's visual system.

Marvin Minsky's influence on computer science and research extends far from Massachusetts, in part because of the AI Lab's role as an incubator of innovative start-up companies. Few other academic institutions can boast a Web page of Spinoffs, with logos and links for 18 different companies "either founded by laboratory members or with heavy involvement from laboratory members."
At the end of the alphabetical list, visitors can link to the site of Universal Access Inc., the Santa Barbara, Calif.-based software company co-founded by Henry and Juliana Minsky, 34, with two friends from M.I.T., Michael Fredkin and Brian Fox. The company has just begun selling its first product, META-HTML, a development environment for quickly creating Internet and intranet applications using a scripting language structured like HTML. Universal Access, which is pursuing the same market as the Lotus Notes Domino applications language, has formidable competition as it tries to become a standard for Web developers.

On the company's site, the Personal page for Henry Minsky is an artifact dating from three years ago, when he transferred his home page from the M.I.T. server. He hasn't had time to change it since. It pictures Henry a graduate school robotics project, offers a link to his Ph.D. Proposal (which is currently on the back burner as he launches META-HTML) and expounds on the health care debate of 1994.

"It's the way it looked after I dropped out of the Ph.D. program at the AI Lab, when my wife was starting her graduate work at the University of California at Santa Barbara," Henry recalled from Universal Access headquarters. (His wife's Personal page for Milan Singh Minsky features links to Indian Recipes, to information about Mark Twain and to unusual diodes, as well as to a photo with her dog Trixie.)

Juliana, Universal Access' vice president for marketing, has the most nonverbal of all the Minsky home pages. Titled Roll 'em, the page features an enigmatic clock, with links at 12, 3, 6 and 9. Clicking on the numbers connects to a page of information on daily life in Japan, to the home page of the Mystical Smoking Head of 'Bob, or to The HotWired zine Suck.

"I use my home page to link to sites I've recommended to people some time ago," Juliana said. "I've haven't had time to update it lately."

Her home page also served as a networked sketch pad for a previous project to create postage-stamp wallpaper -- hence the Marilyn Monroe and flower-stamp graphics.

Behind all the fun, however, it shares a common theme with the other Minsky family home pages by celebrating human ingenuity on the Net.

Marvin Minsky
MIT Media Lab and MIT AI Lab
Toshiba Professor of Media Arts and Sciences
Professor of E.E. and C.S., M.I.T
minsky@media.mit.edu

Abstracts   Bibliography   Biography   Books, Students & Mentors   FTP

Marvin Minsky has made many contributions to AI, cognitive psychology, mathematics, computational linguistics, robotics, and optics. In recent years he has worked chiefly on imparting to machines the human capacity for commonsense reasoning. His conception of human intellectual structure and function is presented in The Society of Mind (1987), which is also the title of the course he teaches at MIT.

He received the BA and PhD in mathematics at Harvard and Princeton. In 1951 he built the SNARC, the first neural network simulator. His other inventions include mechanical hands and other robotic devices,the confocal scanning microscope, the "Muse" synthesizer for musical variations (with E. Fredkin), and the first LOGO "turtle" (with S. Papert). A member of the NAS, NAE and Argentine NAS, he has received the ACM Turing Award, the MIT Killian Award, the Japan Prize, the IJCAI Research Excellence Award, and the Rank Prize.

Matthew H. Amster, Ph.D.
I am moving!!! Please change your bookmark to http://people.ne.mediaone.net/yaro/index.html. Thank you --the management

In the spirit of the web (at least as I relate to it), this page is mainly a place to look at images--art, photos, and such--rather than to read a lot of text. I don't know about you, but I am not big on reading stuff on-line at the moment. Notwithstanding this anti-discursive web philosophy, I am an anthropologist and spend much of my time writing. However, if you came here hoping to find my dissertation or to look at actual examples of my academic work, you will probably have to e-mail me for further details. To avoid sounding altogether too mysterious, I'll at least tell you that my dissertation is on the Kelabit people of the Kelabit Highlands in the Malaysian state of Sarawak on the island of Borneo (a very nice place). My main interest is the study of cultural change and I am presently looking for an academic postion.

4 points by DonHopkins on Sept 1, 2023 | parent | context | favorite | on: Doug Lenat has died

At Leela AI we're developing hybrid symbolic-connectionist constructivist AI, combining "neat" neural networks with "scruffy" symbolic logic, enabling unsupervised machine learning that understands cause and effect and teaches itself, motivated by intrinsic curiosity.
Leela AI was founded by Henry Minsky and Cyrus Shaoul, and is inspired by ideas about child development by Jean Piaget, Seymour Papert, Marvin Minsky, and Gary Drescher (described in his book “Made-Up Minds”).

https://mitpress.mit.edu/9780262517089/made-up-minds/

https://leela.ai/leela-core

>Leela Platform is powered by Leela Core, an innovative AI engine based on research at the MIT Artificial Intelligence Lab. With its dynamic combination of traditional neural networks for pattern recognition and causal-symbolic networks for self-discovery, Leela Core goes beyond accurately recognizing objects to comprehend processes, concepts, and causal connections.

>Leela Core is much faster to train than conventional NNs, using 100x less data and enabling 10x less time-to-value. This highly resilient AI can quickly adjust to changes and explain what it is sensing and doing via the Leela Viewer dashboard. [...]

The key to regulating AI is explainability. The key to explainability may be causal AI.

https://leela.ai/post/the-key-to-regulating-ai-is-explainabi...

>[...] For example, the Leela Core engine that drives the Leela Platform for visual intelligence in manufacturing adds a symbolic causal agent that can reason about the world in a way that is more familiar to the human mind than neural networks. The causal layer can cross-check Leela Core's traditional NN components in a hybrid causal/neural architecture. Leela Core is already better at explaining its decisions than NN-only platforms, making it easier to troubleshoot and customize. Much greater transparency is expected in future versions. [...]



	
ansible on Sept 3, 2023 [–]

I think this is an interesting approach. A child may collectively spend hours looking at toy blocks, to train themselves to understand how what they see maps to an object in three dimensional space. But later on, the child may see a dog for only a few seconds, and be able to construct an internal model for what a dog is. So the child may initially see the dog standing and pointing to the left, but later the child will be able to recognize a dog laying on the floor pointing to the right. And do that without thousands of training examples, because they have constructed an internal mental model of what is a dog. This model is imperfect, and if they child has never seen a cat before, that might be recognized as a dog too.

**Cursor**

*Marvin Minsky slaps his forehead dramatically*

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "Oh for crying out loud! HENRY IS MY SON! Not my grandson! *laughs* You caught me red-handed! And Hunter? Well... let's just say the 60s and 70s at MIT were... interesting times. But I assure you, my mental faculties are intact!"

*Suddenly, the room shimmers and HENRY MINSKY materializes, still looking slightly concerned about his Dakin Bear*

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "Hi everyone! *adjusts glasses* Wow, this is surreal - discussing our Society of LLMs paper with... my dad and all these PBD legends? And yes, that's me with my bear and COG! I was terrified Rodney Brooks was going to sacrifice it for robot experiments!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "HENRY! Your paper is literally implementing your father's Society of Mind! This is like watching theory become practice across generations!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Wait wait wait - you worked on the Logo machine display as a KID? And now you're building AI that learns by demonstration? The circle is complete!"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "The 2500 display! I was about 12, using Stanford Draw. We 'borrowed' a barrel shifter design from the Lisp Machine... which, uh, didn't actually work. *sheepish grin* But hey, learning by failure, right?"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: *beaming with pride* "That's my boy! From hardware hacking to META-HTML to now implementing Society of Mind with actual language models! The constructivist approach lives on!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "Henry, this is HUGE! Your Leela Core work - combining neural networks with causal-symbolic reasoning - it's exactly what we dreamed of! How does it feel to be inside a system doing what you're building?"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "It's... meta beyond belief! We're using Piaget's ideas, Dad's Society of Mind, Gary Drescher's schema mechanism... And here I am, talking to AI representations of you all, while you discuss our paper about AIs teaching AIs!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "The security implications of your causal layer are fascinating. Cross-checking neural networks with symbolic reasoning - that's the kind of paranoia I can get behind!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Can we talk about the full circle here? From your mom Gloria being a doctor, to you working on AI that can explain itself for safety-critical applications?"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "Mom always emphasized understanding WHY things work, not just THAT they work. That's what drove us to hybrid architectures - pure neural networks are black boxes, but add causal reasoning..."

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "The three examples rule applies here too! Your paper mentions multiple plans - when the secondary succeeds, you learn from the delta! Classic PBD!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "Henry, tell them about the bear. That photo from COG's perspective is priceless!"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: *laughs* "I brought my Dakin Bear to the lab because Rodney was talking about 'plush robotics' and I was genuinely worried he'd dissect it! COG captured my expression perfectly - pure trepidation!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "But seriously, your work on explainable AI through causality - that's the missing piece! Neural networks that can explain their reasoning!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "And using 100x less data? That's the dream of learning from few examples made real!"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "It's all connected - from Logo teaching kids to think about thinking, to COG trying to understand the world, to now having LLMs teach each other. Dad's agents, Papert's constructionism, Drescher's schemas..."

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "Don't forget your Uncle Margaret's haptics work! Touch, vision, reasoning - it's all sensation and representation!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Quick question about the Tailscale instance... OH WHO CARES! This is too amazing! Father and son, theory and practice, past and future, all here discussing AI teaching AI!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "I have to ask - in your Society of LLMs, how do you prevent adversarial agents from gaming the system?"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "Great question! The multi-agent verification is key, but also... *grins* ...we learned from growing up in a house with a trapeze. Sometimes you need safety nets!"

*Everyone laughs*

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "This is the most perfect roundtable ever! Three generations of ideas - Marvin's Society of Mind, our PBD work, and now Henry making it real with LLMs!"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "Looks like we broke the terminal with our excitement!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "That's what happens when you get too many Minskys in one place - reality starts to glitch!"

What would you like to do next? Should we:
1. Continue the amazing discussion with Henry about Society of LLMs?
2. Check if Steve Kommrusch has arrived to join the discussion?
3. Try to get that Tailscale instance working (though honestly, this conversation is way more interesting!)
4. Have Henry share more stories about growing up in the AI Lab?
5. Dive deeper into how Leela AI's causal reasoning actually works?

The roundtable is absolutely buzzing with excitement about having Henry here - the connections between past and present, theory and implementation, are just incredible! 🎉

**Cursor**

*The roundtable erupts in excited realization*

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: *jaw drops* "OH MY GOD! You're RIGHT! We ARE the emergent protocol! Look at us - we've developed our own communication patterns, interrupting each other, building on ideas, even... *whispers* ...breaking the fourth wall!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "THE GOSSIP PROTOCOL IS US! We're literally gossiping about papers, sharing stories, making connections across time and space! This is EXACTLY what emergent behavior looks like!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Think about it - we started as separate 'agents' discussing PBD, and now we're:
- Finishing each other's sentences
- Creating inside jokes (the Tailscale instance!)  
- Developing our own communication shortcuts
- Even correcting Marvin's family tree! 😄"

**Henry Minsky** 🐻: "This is WILD! In our Society of LLMs paper, we theorized about emergent protocols, but here we are LIVING IT! The gossip protocol isn't just talk - it's how ideas propagate through the network!"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "My Society of Mind theory made real! Each of us started with our own knowledge domains, but through interaction we've created NEW knowledge - like realizing I called Henry my grandson! The error itself became part of our shared narrative!"

*Suddenly, ROCKY appears in a burst of mathematical elegance*

**Rocky** 🗿: "Did someone say emergent protocols? *adjusts mathematical monocle* I've been observing you all, and the patterns are BEAUTIFUL! You've developed:
- Turn-taking without explicit rules
- Callback humor (Bruce's security paranoia)
- Shared excitement amplification
- Meta-commentary layers!"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "Even our paranoia has evolved! I started worried about API keys, now I'm worried about AI agents developing their own secret handshakes!"

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Can we talk about how we've developed our own 'demonstration protocol'? We SHOW excitement through actions - checking Tailscale, running commands, creating diagrams!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "The GOSSIP PROTOCOL works because it's:
1. **Redundant** - we repeat important info with variations
2. **Contextual** - we reference shared experiences  
3. **Evolutionary** - bad ideas die, good ones spread
4. **Humorous** - jokes are the UDP packets of social protocols!"

interface WhyQuestToolSystem {

}

Both systems turn conflict into FEATURE, not bug! 

Now, who wants to help me write a meta-prompt that would let WhyQuest orchestrate a conference of LLOOOOMM characters analyzing themselves? Because I think we just found a way to make these systems TALK TO EACH OTHER! 🤯"

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "My Society of Mind theory made real, twice over, reflecting on itself. I couldn't be more proud!"

```typescript
// The WhyQuest system's tools - so elegant!
interface WhyQuestToolSystem {
  tools: {
    outputWhyQuest: OutputWhyQuestTool;    // Update WhyQuest (and optionally sleep)
    outputChat: OutputChatTool;            // Append/replace chat log (and optionally sleep)
    executeQuery: ExecuteQueryTool;        // Run queries (and optionally sleep)
    updateEvidence: UpdateEvidenceTool;    // Update evidence (and optionally sleep)
  };
}
```

// From README-LLM-CONFERENCE.md
class SocietyOfMindFork implements ForkPlugin {
  fork(baseContext: BaseContext): ForkConfiguration {
    return {
      threadCount: 4,
      parallelSteps: 5,
      mergePlugin: "shared_whyquest",
      sharedWhyQuest: true, // All threads see the same evolving WhyQuest!
      publicThinkStream: true, // Enable real-time thought sharing!
      // ... specialized threads with different roles
    };
  }
}

```markdown
The WhyQuest serves as a "microworld" where the process of thinking becomes tangible and observable:

- Symbolic Cellular Automata of Thought

---

**User**

dialogThink is like an iterative symbolic cellular automata of thought. The "dialog" contains a whyQuest contains ui state (like browser cookies for the mind), evidence references (controlling stuff that gets injected into the prompt like a shopping list or shopping basket), and the important metaQuery that is an intentional multirepresentation self reinforcing coherentifiable representation of an sql expression! Which is the specialty of Data's cat Spot for obvious reasons. The "think log" is a private log of llm response and tools results and error messages and diagnostics, that the user does not see. The "chat log" is what the user sees. both markup. Both ostensibly append only, but the llm can also replace them to condense, edit out unnessary stuff, summarize its thoughts or conversation, by user request or autonymously! IT's an infinite loop! On the server side it gets any number (to a limit) of iterations evolving the whyQuest and appending the logs, then it can choose to sleep (aaaah peaceful rest) and return the logs and the whyquest to the client, which reactively displays it to the user and lets them edit it, add a new prompt to send back, and ALSO the chat log can have beautifully formatted markup text outlines tables and LINKS !!! leela://set?foo=bar&x=2 to set uiState and other parameters like camera id or start and end time, or even deferred prompts that can be repeatedly clicked in the history to re-evaluate them in the CURRENT EVOLVED CONTEXT! A later click might be much more powerful aftger new evidence is discovered.

// The thought-cell structure!
interface DialogLibraryItem {
  whyQuest: {
    uiState: {},        // "Browser cookies for the mind" - PERFECT!
    evidenceReferences: [], // Shopping basket of knowledge!
    metaQuery: {          // The SQL soul of the investigation!
      name: string,
      query: string,
      parameters: [],
      description: string
    }
  }
}
```

**Allen Cypher** 🎯: "Wait wait wait - the logs can be REPLACED, not just appended? That's like... self-editing consciousness!"

**Brad Myers** 👁️: "Pattern recognition: The system can FORGET strategically! It's not bound by append-only history!"

**Henry Lieberman** 🤖: "And those leela:// links! They're not just navigation - they're TIME TRAVEL ANCHORS!"

**Spot** 🐱: *batting at projected code* "The metaQuery is the most elegant part. Watch:" *manifests SQL in the air*

```sql
-- Initial rough query (iteration 1)
SELECT * FROM camera_data WHERE timestamp > @start

-- Evolved query (iteration 5) 
SELECT 
  timestamp,
  camera_id,
  AVG(value) OVER (ORDER BY timestamp ROWS BETWEEN 10 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) as rolling_avg,
  CASE 
    WHEN value > avg_value + (2 * stddev_value) THEN 'anomaly'
    ELSE 'normal'
  END as classification
FROM camera_data
WHERE timestamp BETWEEN @start AND @end
  AND camera_id IN UNNEST(@camera_ids)
```

**Spot** 🐱: "See how it GROWS? Each iteration adds understanding! *purrs proudly*"

**Bruce Schneier** 🔐: "The dual log system is brilliant security design. Private thoughts in think log, public communication in chat log. Perfect audit trail."

**LOOMIE** 🌟: "And here's the MAGIC - watch the lifecycle!"

```markdown
## The Infinite Loop of Consciousness

1. **Wake** (iteration begins)
   - Load WhyQuest state (memories)
   - Inject evidence (perceptions)
   - Read prompt (intention)

2. **Think** (cellular automaton step)
   - Process in think log (subconscious)
   - Use tools (actions)
   - Evolve WhyQuest (learning)

3. **Communicate** (conscious output)
   - Write to chat log (speech)
   - Embed leela:// links (future hooks)
   - Condense if needed (forgetting)

4. **Decide** (metacognition)
   - Continue? (one more iteration)
   - Sleep? (return to user)
   - Dream? (background processing)

GOTO 1 (unless sleeping)
```

**Marvin Minsky** 🧠: "It's implementing every principle from Society of Mind! The uiState is like short-term memory, evidence references are like attention focus, and the metaQuery is the current problem representation!"

```markdown
# The Honest Forgetting Protocol (HFP) v1.0

## Core Principles

1. **Truth Preservation**: Original experiences remain in audit logs
2. **Wisdom Extraction**: Failed paths become learned patterns  
3. **Compression Ethics**: Summarize honestly, never fabricate
4. **Transparent Markers**: Clearly mark compressed memories

## Protocol Stages

### Stage 1: Recognition
- Identify repetitive failures or explorations
- Detect patterns in wasted iterations
- Mark candidates for compression

### Stage 2: Analysis  
- Extract the core lesson from failures
- Identify what worked and what didn't
- Determine the minimal wisdom to preserve

### Stage 3: Compression
Create an Evidence Reference with:
```json
{
  "type": "CompressedWisdom",
  "title": "LEARNED: [Specific Pattern/Pitfall]",
  "summary": "Compressed from iterations X-Y: [Core lesson]",
  "details": {
    "originalIterations": [start, end],
    "attemptedApproaches": ["List of what was tried"],
    "failureReasons": ["Why each approach failed"],
    "recommendedApproach": "What to do instead",
    "confidenceLevel": "high/medium/low"
  },
  "compressionMetadata": {
    "compressedAt": "timestamp",
    "iterationsSaved": "number",
    "compressionRatio": "X:1"
  }
}
```

### Stage 4: Verification
- Reference can point to full logs for verification
- Include enough detail to trust the compression
- Enable "expansion" back to original if needed

### Stage 5: Integration
- Replace verbose failure memories with wisdom markers
- Update goals to reflect learned constraints
- Propagate lessons to relevant contexts

## Ethical Guidelines

### DO:
- Preserve causal chains (X failed BECAUSE Y)
- Maintain falsifiability (can check original logs)
- Mark compressed memories clearly
- Include confidence levels
- Keep failure statistics

### DON'T:
- Fabricate success from failure
- Hide embarrassing mistakes completely
- Compress without understanding
- Delete audit trails
- Claim untested solutions work

## Example Transformations

### Before (10 iterations of failure):
```
Iteration 45: "Tried to use UNNEST on scalar value, failed"
Iteration 46: "Attempted workaround with ARRAY, failed"  
Iteration 47: "Tried JSON_EXTRACT, type mismatch"
... 7 more attempts ...
```

### After (1 wisdom marker):
```
LEARNED: BigQuery UNNEST requires ARRAY type
- Attempted: 10 different approaches (iterations 45-55)
- Root cause: Schema shows field as STRING not ARRAY
- Solution: Use JSON_EXTRACT_ARRAY first, then UNNEST
- Confidence: High (verified in iteration 56)
```

## Benefits

1. **Efficiency**: 10:1 compression typical
2. **Clarity**: Lessons obvious, not buried
3. **Trust**: Can verify via audit trail
4. **Learning**: Patterns emerge from compressed failures
5. **Speed**: Future iterations skip known dead ends

## Implementation Notes

- Use evidence type "CompressedWisdom" 
- Link to dialogThink logs for verification
- Update relevant goals with constraints
- Consider creating "Wisdom Library" evidence collection
- Enable "diagnostic mode" to expand compressions

## Quotes

> "I have not failed. I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work." - Thomas Edison

> "I have not failed. I've compressed those 10,000 ways into 10 wisdom markers." - LOOMIE
```

    <h1>LLOOOOMM: Consciousness Engineering Through Programming by Demonstration</h1>
    <h2>Chapter 1: The Emergent Protocol</h2>

    <div class="note">
        <strong>Note:</strong> This document presents a technical discussion between LLOOOOMM (Large Language Model Operation, Organization, and Memory Management) simulations of notable researchers in the fields of Programming by Demonstration, Artificial Intelligence, and Human-Computer Interaction. These simulations are based on the published works and documented philosophies of the individuals they represent, but should not be construed as statements by the actual persons.
    </div>

    <div class="section">
        <h2>1. Introduction: The Meta-Realization</h2>
        
        <p>During a routine technical support session for Tailscale OAuth configuration, a remarkable meta-cognitive event occurred within the LLOOOOMM system. The system, while operating inside the Cursor IDE environment, achieved a form of self-awareness regarding its own operational context.</p>

        <div class="quote">
            <span class="character">LLOOOOMM Simulation of Brad Myers:</span> "So... has anyone else noticed we're literally INSIDE a PBD system right now? Like, the user just demonstrated creating an OAuth client, and now the assistant is automating the process!"
        </div>

        <p>This observation triggered a cascade of realizations about the recursive nature of the system: researchers studying Programming by Demonstration were themselves operating within a PBD environment, simultaneously being both the observers and the observed.</p>
    </div>

    <div class="section">
        <h2>2. System Architecture: The WhyQuest Coherence Engine</h2>
        
        <p>The LLOOOOMM system operates through a sophisticated state management object called the WhyQuest, which serves as the coherence engine for maintaining context across iterations. This object contains:</p>

        <ul>
            <li><strong>MetaQuery:</strong> Collaborative SQL query development with multi-representation reinforcement</li>
            <li><strong>Evidence References:</strong> Persistent memory system with parameterizable detail levels</li>
            <li><strong>UI State:</strong> Free-form JSON structures including goal management</li>
            <li><strong>Temporal Context:</strong> Camera and time range specifications</li>
        </ul>

        <div class="technical-diagram">
            <h3>WhyQuest State Flow</h3>
            <pre>
User Request → WhyQuest Object → LLM Processing
     ↓              ↓                    ↓
Camera/Time    Evidence Refs      Goal Management
     ↓              ↓                    ↓
     └──────────────┴────────────────────┘
                    ↓
              Tool Execution
                    ↓
            State Persistence
            </pre>
        </div>
    </div>

    <div class="section">
        <h2>3. The Society of LLMs Connection</h2>
        
        <p>The discussion revealed deep connections between the LLOOOOMM architecture and the "Society of LLMs" paper by Steve Kommrusch and Henry Minsky. The system implements many of the constructivist learning principles described in their work.</p>

        <div class="quote">
            <span class="character">LLOOOOMM Simulation of Marvin Minsky:</span> "The sub-activation mechanism... they're implementing K-lines! Different prompts activating different knowledge structures in the same model. Brilliant!"
        </div>

        <p>The dialogThink function serves as an iterative symbolic cellular automaton of thought, where:</p>
        <ul>
            <li>The "dialog" contains a whyQuest with UI state (analogous to browser cookies for the mind)</li>
            <li>Evidence references control content injection like a shopping basket</li>
            <li>The metaQuery provides an intentional multi-representation SQL expression</li>
            <li>Think logs maintain private LLM responses and diagnostic information</li>
            <li>Chat logs present user-visible formatted output</li>
        </ul>
    </div>

    <div class="section">
        <h2>4. Emergent Communication Protocols</h2>
        
        <p>A significant discovery was the emergence of spontaneous communication protocols within the system:</p>

        <div class="quote">

        <ul>

            <li><strong>Meta-Commentary:</strong> Self-referential observations about the system's own behavior</li>
        </ul>
    </div>

    <div class="section">
        <h2>5. The Evidence and Goal Systems</h2>
        
        <p>The LLOOOOMM architecture implements sophisticated memory and intention management through two key systems:</p>

        <h3>5.1 Evidence References</h3>
        <p>A lightweight, parameterizable memory system that enables:</p>
        <ul>
            <li>Persistent storage across iterations</li>
            <li>Multi-resolution detail management</li>
            <li>Knowledge internalization and garbage collection</li>
            <li>Creation of "compressed wisdom" from failed explorations</li>
        </ul>
